4
Chapter 4  ·  Statistical Inference

Hypothesis Testing

From null hypotheses to p-values — the logic of statistical decisions

Learning Objectives

  • Formulate null and alternative hypotheses and understand the logical structure of Neyman-Pearson testing
  • Compute and interpret p-values, critical values, and rejection regions for a variety of test statistics
  • Derive and apply the Neyman-Pearson Lemma to construct most powerful tests
  • Perform z-tests, t-tests (one-sample, two-sample, paired), chi-square tests, and F-tests with full derivation of test statistics
  • Apply the generalized likelihood ratio test and Wilks' theorem to construct large-sample tests
  • Conduct goodness-of-fit tests (chi-square, G-test) and tests for independence in contingency tables
  • Apply non-parametric alternatives including the Mann-Whitney U and Wilcoxon signed-rank tests
  • Perform power analysis, compute sample sizes, and interpret effect sizes (Cohen's d, OR, RR)
  • Understand and apply multiple testing corrections: Bonferroni, Holm, and Benjamini-Hochberg FDR
  • Construct and interpret diagnostic plots: Q-Q plots, hanging rootograms, and normality tests

4.1 The Logic of Hypothesis Testing

Statistical hypothesis testing is the formal machinery by which we use data to make principled decisions about competing models of reality. Unlike estimation, which asks “what is the value of this parameter?”, testing asks the sharper question: “is the data consistent with a specific claim about the parameter?” This distinction — between summarizing evidence and making binary decisions — defines the entire enterprise of null hypothesis significance testing (NHST).

The framework was developed jointly by Ronald A. Fisher, who introduced the p-value and the notion of evidence against a null hypothesis, and by Jerzy Neyman and Egon Pearson, who formalized the decision-theoretic structure of error rates, power, and critical regions. These two philosophies remain philosophically distinct but practically intertwined in modern statistics. We will develop both strands carefully throughout this chapter.

Before any formal machinery, it is worth appreciating the fundamental asymmetry that lies at the heart of NHST: we never “prove” the null hypothesis — we only fail to reject it. This asymmetry is deliberate and profound, rooted in the same logic as proof by contradiction in mathematics.

4.1.1 What Is a Hypothesis?

Definition · Statistical Hypothesis

A statistical hypothesis is a statement about the distribution of one or more random variables. Formally, let $\mathcal{P} = \{P_\theta : \theta \in \Theta\}$ be a parametric family indexed by $\theta \in \Theta \subseteq \mathbb{R}^k$. A hypothesis specifies a restriction on $\theta$:

$$H : \theta \in \Theta_0 \subseteq \Theta.$$

A hypothesis is simple if $\Theta_0 = \{\theta_0\}$ (a single point) and composite if $|\Theta_0| > 1$. A parametric hypothesis concerns $\theta$ in a finite-dimensional family; a non-parametric hypothesis concerns the distribution $P$ without a finite-dimensional parameterization.

In practice, we always operate with two competing hypotheses. The null hypothesis $H_0$ represents the baseline or “status quo” claim — typically a specific point or region of parameter space that embodies “no effect,” “no difference,” or a known reference value. The alternative hypothesis $H_1$ (or $H_a$) represents the departure we wish to detect.

Definition · Null and Alternative Hypotheses

Given the parameter space $\Theta$, a null hypothesis $H_0: \theta \in \Theta_0$ and an alternative hypothesis $H_1: \theta \in \Theta_1$ form a hypothesis pair if $\Theta_0 \cap \Theta_1 = \emptyset$. The alternative is

  • One-sided (right): $H_1: \theta > \theta_0$, so $\Theta_1 = (\theta_0, \infty)$
  • One-sided (left): $H_1: \theta < \theta_0$, so $\Theta_1 = (-\infty, \theta_0)$
  • Two-sided: $H_1: \theta \neq \theta_0$, so $\Theta_1 = \mathbb{R} \setminus \{\theta_0\}$

4.1.2 The Logical Structure of NHST

The logic of NHST is precisely that of indirect proof (reductio ad absurdum). We assume $H_0$ is true and compute the probability of observing data as extreme as (or more extreme than) what we actually observed. If this probability is very small, we conclude that the data are inconsistent with $H_0$ and we “reject” it.

Historically, Fisher viewed the p-value as a continuous measure of evidence against $H_0$: the smaller the p-value, the stronger the evidence against the null. Under this view, no pre-specified threshold is required — the researcher examines the p-value and makes a contextual judgment. Neyman and Pearson, by contrast, emphasized the decision-theoretic structure: one chooses a significance level $\alpha$ before the experiment, and the test is a formal decision rule that controls error rates in repeated experiments.

Intuition · Why We Cannot “Prove” the Null

Consider the analogy: “All swans are white” is the null hypothesis. Observing 1,000 white swans does not prove it — but a single black swan disproves it. NHST works analogously: we accumulate evidence against $H_0$, not evidence for it. A failure to reject means only that the data are insufficient to contradict $H_0$, not that $H_0$ is true. This asymmetry has profound consequences for scientific inference and replication.

Warning · Accepting vs. Failing to Reject

A common error is to say “we accept $H_0$” when a test is non-significant. The correct statement is: “we fail to reject $H_0$ at level $\alpha$.” Accepting implies evidence for the null; failing to reject merely acknowledges insufficient evidence against it. This distinction is critical in regulatory settings, clinical trials, and any context where a Type II error carries real cost.

Example · Formulating Hypotheses in Biostatistics

Setup (Harvard-level Biostatistics): A clinical trial tests whether a new cholesterol drug reduces mean LDL by more than 10 mg/dL compared to placebo. Baseline mean for placebo is $\mu_0 = 130$ mg/dL. Let $\mu$ denote the population mean LDL under the new treatment.

Hypotheses: We test $$H_0: \mu \geq 120 \quad \text{vs.} \quad H_1: \mu < 120.$$ This is a one-sided, composite-vs-composite test. A reduction of at least 10 mg/dL corresponds to the alternative $\mu < 120$, so we reject $H_0$ only if the sample mean is sufficiently small.

Key point: The hypothesis is on the population mean $\mu$ — an unknown parameter — not on the sample mean $\bar{X}$. The sample mean $\bar{X}$ is the data; $\mu$ is what we are testing.

4.1.3 Two Philosophies of Testing

The Fisherian and Neyman–Pearson frameworks differ in fundamental ways, though modern statistical practice blends them:

  • Fisher: Use the p-value as a continuous measure of evidence. No pre-specified $\alpha$; report the p-value and let the scientific community judge. The null hypothesis is the only hypothesis under consideration — there is no formal alternative and hence no concept of power.
  • Neyman–Pearson: Pre-specify $\alpha$ (the maximum tolerable Type I error rate) before collecting data. Design the test to maximize power (minimize Type II error) at that level. The alternative hypothesis $H_1$ is explicitly specified and power depends on where $\theta$ lies in $\Theta_1$.

In practice, most scientists report the p-value (Fisher) but compare it to a pre-specified threshold $\alpha = 0.05$ (Neyman–Pearson), a hybrid that satisfies neither camp fully but has proven enormously useful.

4.2 Rejection Regions, Critical Values, and p-Values

Given a hypothesis pair $(H_0, H_1)$, a test is a decision rule: a function mapping the observed data $\mathbf{X} = (X_1, \ldots, X_n)$ to one of two actions — reject $H_0$ or retain $H_0$. This decision is based on a test statistic $T = T(\mathbf{X})$, a scalar summary of the data chosen to be sensitive to departures from $H_0$ in the direction of $H_1$.

4.2.1 The Rejection Region

Definition · Test and Rejection Region

A nonrandomized test is defined by its rejection region (or critical region) $\mathcal{R} \subseteq \mathbb{R}^n$. We reject $H_0$ if and only if $\mathbf{X} \in \mathcal{R}$. Equivalently, in terms of a test statistic $T(\mathbf{X})$:

$$\delta(\mathbf{X}) = \begin{cases} 1 & T(\mathbf{X}) \in C \\ 0 & T(\mathbf{X}) \notin C \end{cases}$$

where $C \subseteq \mathbb{R}$ is the critical region for $T$. For a right-sided test, $C = (c_\alpha, \infty)$; for a left-sided test, $C = (-\infty, c_\alpha)$; for a two-sided test, $C = (-\infty, -c_{\alpha/2}) \cup (c_{\alpha/2}, \infty)$.

4.2.2 Size, Level, and Critical Values

Definition · Size, Level, and Critical Value

The size of a test $\delta$ is $$\alpha(\delta) = \sup_{\theta \in \Theta_0} \mathbb{P}_\theta(T(\mathbf{X}) \in C).$$ A test has level $\alpha$ if $\alpha(\delta) \leq \alpha$. The critical value $c_\alpha$ satisfies $$\mathbb{P}_{H_0}(T > c_\alpha) = \alpha$$ for a right-sided test, so that $c_\alpha = F_T^{-1}(1 - \alpha)$ where $F_T$ is the CDF of $T$ under $H_0$.

The critical value marks the boundary between the rejection and acceptance regions. For a two-sided test at level $\alpha$, the two critical values are $\pm c_{\alpha/2}$, and the rejection region is $|T| > c_{\alpha/2}$. These are obtained from the quantiles of the null distribution of $T$.

For standard tests, the null distributions and critical values are tabulated:

  • z-test: $T \sim N(0,1)$ under $H_0$; critical values $z_\alpha = \Phi^{-1}(1-\alpha)$, e.g., $z_{0.05} = 1.645$, $z_{0.025} = 1.960$.
  • t-test ($n-1$ df): $T \sim t_{n-1}$ under $H_0$; critical value $t_{n-1,\alpha}$ from Student's t-table.
  • Chi-square test ($k-1$ df): $T \sim \chi^2_{k-1}$ under $H_0$; right-tail critical value $\chi^2_{k-1,1-\alpha}$.
  • F-test ($m,n$ df): $T \sim F_{m,n}$ under $H_0$; right-tail critical value $F_{m,n,1-\alpha}$.

4.2.3 The p-Value: Definition, Properties, and Interpretation

Definition · p-Value

The p-value of an observed test statistic $t_\mathrm{obs} = T(\mathbf{x})$ is the probability, under $H_0$, of observing a value of $T$ at least as extreme as $t_\mathrm{obs}$. For a right-sided test: $$p = \mathbb{P}_{H_0}(T \geq t_\mathrm{obs}).$$ For a left-sided test: $$p = \mathbb{P}_{H_0}(T \leq t_\mathrm{obs}).$$ For a two-sided test (when $T$ has a symmetric null distribution): $$p = 2\min\bigl(\mathbb{P}_{H_0}(T \geq |t_\mathrm{obs}|),\; \mathbb{P}_{H_0}(T \leq -|t_\mathrm{obs}|)\bigr) = 2\mathbb{P}_{H_0}(T \geq |t_\mathrm{obs}|).$$

For continuous $T$ under $H_0$, the p-value $p \sim \mathrm{Uniform}(0,1)$ when $H_0$ is true. This is the fundamental calibration result: a valid p-value has a flat distribution under the null.

Core Result
$$\text{Reject } H_0 \text{ at level } \alpha \iff p\text{-value} \leq \alpha$$

This equivalence between p-value thresholding and critical-value comparison is the key practical relationship. It allows researchers to either (a) compute the test statistic and compare to the critical value, or (b) compute the p-value and compare to $\alpha$ — both procedures produce identical accept/reject decisions.

Figure 4.4. The p-value (red shaded area) is the probability of observing $Z \geq 2.10$ under $H_0: Z \sim N(0,1)$. Here $p = 0.0179$. The gold dotted line marks the critical value at $\alpha = 0.05$. Since $z_{\mathrm{obs}} = 2.10 > z_{0.05} = 1.645$ and $p < 0.05$, we reject $H_0$ at the 5% level.
Warning · The Most Common p-Value Misinterpretation

The p-value is not the probability that $H_0$ is true. It is not $\mathbb{P}(H_0 \mid \text{data})$. It is $\mathbb{P}(\text{data this extreme} \mid H_0 \text{ is true})$ — a frequentist probability about hypothetical repetitions of the experiment under $H_0$, not a posterior probability about hypotheses. Interpreting $p = 0.03$ as “there is a 3% chance the null is true” is a category error that confuses the conditional with its converse (the prosecutor’s fallacy). The correct statement is: “if $H_0$ were true, there is a 3% chance of observing data this extreme by chance alone.”

Example · Computing a p-Value (Stanford-Level Exam Problem)

Setup: A nutrition researcher measures sodium intake (mg/day) in $n = 36$ adults. The sample mean is $\bar{x} = 2{,}412$ and it is known that $\sigma = 480$ mg/day. Test $H_0: \mu = 2{,}300$ against $H_1: \mu \neq 2{,}300$ at level $\alpha = 0.05$.

Test statistic: $$Z = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu_0}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}} = \frac{2412 - 2300}{480/\sqrt{36}} = \frac{112}{80} = 1.40.$$

p-Value (two-sided): $$p = 2\,\mathbb{P}(Z \geq 1.40) = 2(1 - \Phi(1.40)) = 2(1 - 0.9192) = 0.1616.$$

Decision: Since $p = 0.1616 > 0.05$, we fail to reject $H_0$. The data are consistent with $\mu = 2{,}300$ at the 5% level. This does not prove $\mu = 2{,}300$; it means the observed deviation of 112 mg/day is not unusually large given the noise level $\sigma/\sqrt{n} = 80$.

95% CI check: $\bar{x} \pm 1.96 \cdot 80 = (2255, 2569)$. Since 2,300 falls inside this interval, we reach the same conclusion: fail to reject $H_0$. The CI–test duality holds exactly.

4.2.4 Randomized Tests and the Role of Continuity

For discrete test statistics (e.g., counts from a binomial), the CDF has jumps, meaning there may be no rejection region that attains exactly level $\alpha$. A randomized test resolves this by choosing a probability of rejection $\gamma(t)$ at the boundary value $t$:

$$\delta(\mathbf{x}) = \begin{cases} 1 & T(\mathbf{x}) > c \\ \gamma & T(\mathbf{x}) = c \\ 0 & T(\mathbf{x}) < c \end{cases}$$

where $\gamma \in [0,1]$ is chosen so that $\mathbb{E}_{H_0}[\delta(\mathbf{X})] = \alpha$ exactly. Randomized tests attain exact size $\alpha$ but are rarely used in practice since the randomization introduces an element of arbitrariness. Instead, practitioners use conservative tests (level $< \alpha$, size $< \alpha$) or p-value-based approaches.

4.3 Error Types, Power, and the Neyman–Pearson Framework

Neyman and Pearson’s seminal insight was to frame hypothesis testing as a decision problem with two types of possible errors, and to optimize the test’s ability to detect true departures from $H_0$ subject to a constraint on the rate of false positives. This led to the most important theorem in all of classical testing theory.

4.3.1 The Two Types of Error

Definition · Type I and Type II Errors

For a test with decision function $\delta(\mathbf{X}) \in \{0,1\}$:

  • Type I error (false positive): Reject $H_0$ when $H_0$ is true. Probability $= \mathbb{P}_\theta(\delta = 1)$ for $\theta \in \Theta_0$. The size of the test is $\sup_{\theta \in \Theta_0} \mathbb{P}_\theta(\delta = 1)$. We pre-specify that this must not exceed $\alpha$.
  • Type II error (false negative): Fail to reject $H_0$ when $H_1$ is true. Probability $= \mathbb{P}_\theta(\delta = 0) = 1 - \mathbb{P}_\theta(\delta = 1)$ for $\theta \in \Theta_1$. Denoted $\beta(\theta)$.
  • Power: $\pi(\theta) = 1 - \beta(\theta) = \mathbb{P}_\theta(\delta = 1)$ for $\theta \in \Theta_1$. A more powerful test has a smaller Type II error rate.

The fundamental tension in hypothesis testing is that reducing Type I error (by raising the critical value) increases Type II error (reduces power), and vice versa. The Neyman–Pearson approach resolves this by fixing the maximum acceptable Type I error $\alpha$ and then maximizing power over all level-$\alpha$ tests.

Figure 4.1. Null distribution $N(0,1)$ (blue) and alternative distribution $N(\delta/\sigma_n, 1)$ (gold) for the one-sample z-test. The red shaded region is the Type I error (size $\alpha$); the blue shaded region is the Type II error ($\beta$). Power $= 1 - \beta$ is the gold shaded area to the right of the critical value under the alternative. As $n$ increases, the two distributions separate and power rises.

4.3.2 The Power Function

Definition · Power Function

The power function of a test $\delta$ is the map $$\pi_\delta(\theta) = \mathbb{P}_\theta(\delta(\mathbf{X}) = 1), \quad \theta \in \Theta.$$ Note: at $\theta \in \Theta_0$, $\pi_\delta(\theta)$ is the probability of a Type I error; at $\theta \in \Theta_1$, it is the power.

A test with power function $\pi_\delta$ has size $\alpha$ if $\sup_{\theta \in \Theta_0} \pi_\delta(\theta) = \alpha$. We say $\delta^*$ is uniformly most powerful (UMP) at level $\alpha$ if $\pi_{\delta^*}(\theta) \geq \pi_\delta(\theta)$ for all $\theta \in \Theta_1$ and all level-$\alpha$ tests $\delta$.

Figure 4.2. Power function $\pi(\mu)$ of the right-sided z-test $H_0:\mu = 0$ vs. $H_1:\mu > 0$ at level $\alpha = 0.05$ for sample sizes $n = 10, 25, 50$. At $\mu = 0$, $\pi(0) = \alpha = 0.05$ for all curves. Power increases with $|\mu|$ and with $n$.

The power function is a fundamental tool for study design: if we know the effect size we wish to detect ($\theta \in \Theta_1$) and the desired power, we can compute the required sample size $n$. Conversely, given $n$, we can assess the power of the study against specific alternatives.

4.3.3 The Neyman–Pearson Lemma

The most celebrated result in hypothesis testing theory gives the optimal test for simple hypotheses — that is, when both $H_0$ and $H_1$ are single points in the parameter space.

Theorem · Neyman–Pearson Lemma

Let $\mathbf{X}$ have joint density (or PMF) $f(\mathbf{x}; \theta)$ and consider testing $H_0: \theta = \theta_0$ vs. $H_1: \theta = \theta_1$ at level $\alpha$. Define the likelihood ratio: $$\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{f(\mathbf{x}; \theta_1)}{f(\mathbf{x}; \theta_0)}.$$ The most powerful level-$\alpha$ test (MPT) rejects $H_0$ if and only if $\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) > k$, where $k$ is chosen so that $$\mathbb{P}_{\theta_0}\bigl(\Lambda(\mathbf{X}) > k\bigr) = \alpha.$$ At the boundary $\Lambda = k$, a randomized rejection probability $\gamma$ is used to attain size exactly $\alpha$.

Uniqueness: Any other level-$\alpha$ test has strictly smaller power than the NP test, unless it is essentially equivalent to the NP test (agrees with it a.s.).

Proof Sketch

Let $\delta^*$ be the NP test and let $\delta$ be any other level-$\alpha$ test. We want to show $\mathbb{E}_{\theta_1}[\delta^*(\mathbf{X})] \geq \mathbb{E}_{\theta_1}[\delta(\mathbf{X})]$.

Consider the difference $D = \delta^*(\mathbf{x}) - \delta(\mathbf{x})$, which takes values in $\{-1, 0, 1\}$. By construction of $\delta^*$:

  • $D > 0$ (i.e., $\delta^* = 1, \delta = 0$) implies $\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) > k$, hence $f(\mathbf{x}; \theta_1) > k \cdot f(\mathbf{x}; \theta_0)$.
  • $D < 0$ (i.e., $\delta^* = 0, \delta = 1$) implies $\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) < k$, hence $f(\mathbf{x}; \theta_1) < k \cdot f(\mathbf{x}; \theta_0)$.
Therefore $D(\mathbf{x}) \cdot [f(\mathbf{x}; \theta_1) - k \cdot f(\mathbf{x}; \theta_0)] \geq 0$ for all $\mathbf{x}$. Integrating: $$\int D(\mathbf{x})[f(\mathbf{x}; \theta_1) - k \cdot f(\mathbf{x}; \theta_0)]\, d\mathbf{x} \geq 0,$$ i.e., $\mathbb{E}_{\theta_1}[\delta^*] - \mathbb{E}_{\theta_1}[\delta] \geq k(\mathbb{E}_{\theta_0}[\delta^*] - \mathbb{E}_{\theta_0}[\delta])$. Since both tests have level $\leq \alpha$ and $k \geq 0$, the right side is $\geq 0$, proving $\delta^*$ has at least as much power as $\delta$. $\blacksquare$

Example · NP Lemma for the Normal Mean (MIT-level Problem)

Setup: $X_1, \ldots, X_n \overset{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim} N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ with $\sigma^2$ known. Test $H_0: \mu = \mu_0$ vs. $H_1: \mu = \mu_1 > \mu_0$.

Likelihood ratio: $$\Lambda(\mathbf{x}) = \frac{\prod_i \phi\left(\frac{x_i - \mu_1}{\sigma}\right)}{\prod_i \phi\left(\frac{x_i - \mu_0}{\sigma}\right)} = \exp\!\left(\frac{\mu_1 - \mu_0}{\sigma^2} \sum_i x_i - \frac{n(\mu_1^2 - \mu_0^2)}{2\sigma^2}\right).$$

Since $\mu_1 > \mu_0$, rejecting when $\Lambda > k$ is equivalent to rejecting when $\bar{X} > c$ for some $c$. The critical value is $c = \mu_0 + z_\alpha \cdot \sigma/\sqrt{n}$, giving the familiar z-test. Thus the one-sided z-test is the most powerful level-$\alpha$ test for this problem — no other test can achieve higher power against $\mu_1$.

4.3.4 Uniformly Most Powerful Tests

The NP lemma applies to simple hypotheses. For composite alternatives, we seek a uniformly most powerful (UMP) test: a test that is simultaneously most powerful against every $\theta \in \Theta_1$.

Definition · Monotone Likelihood Ratio (MLR)

A family $\{f(x; \theta)\}_\theta$ has a monotone likelihood ratio (MLR) in a statistic $T(\mathbf{x})$ if, for all $\theta_1 > \theta_0$, the ratio $f(\mathbf{x}; \theta_1)/f(\mathbf{x}; \theta_0)$ is a monotonically non-decreasing function of $T(\mathbf{x})$.

Theorem · UMP Test for One-Sided MLR Families

If $f(x; \theta)$ has an MLR in $T(\mathbf{x})$ and we test $H_0: \theta \leq \theta_0$ vs. $H_1: \theta > \theta_0$, then the test that rejects when $T(\mathbf{x}) > c_\alpha$ (with $\mathbb{P}_{\theta_0}(T > c_\alpha) = \alpha$) is the UMP level-$\alpha$ test. Furthermore, its power function $\pi(\theta)$ is strictly increasing in $\theta$.

All one-parameter exponential families have the MLR property, which is why z-tests, t-tests, and Poisson tests all yield UMP tests for one-sided hypotheses. For two-sided hypotheses, UMP tests rarely exist (except in special cases), and we instead look for uniformly most powerful unbiased (UMPU) or other restricted-class optima.

Data Science Application · A/B Testing and the NP Framework

In A/B testing (randomized controlled experiments), the NP framework dictates how to design experiments:

  • Set $\alpha$: Typically $\alpha = 0.05$ or $0.01$. This controls the false positive rate — how often you incorrectly conclude a variant is better when it is not.
  • Specify $H_1$: The minimum detectable effect (MDE) you care about. E.g., “We want to detect a 2% lift in click-through rate.”
  • Power target: Usually $1-\beta = 0.80$ or $0.90$, determining how often you correctly detect a true effect.
  • Sample size: Derived from $\alpha$, power, and MDE using the power function of the z-test.

Running an experiment without pre-specifying these parameters leads to “peeking” and inflation of the Type I error rate far above the nominal $\alpha$.

4.4 The Duality Between Confidence Intervals and Hypothesis Tests

One of the most elegant unifying principles in mathematical statistics is the exact duality between confidence intervals and hypothesis tests. Every confidence interval corresponds to a family of hypothesis tests, and every collection of related hypothesis tests corresponds to a confidence interval. Understanding this duality deepens intuition for both concepts and is practically useful for reporting results.

Definition · Confidence Interval via Test Inversion

Let $\{\delta_{\theta_0} : \theta_0 \in \Theta\}$ be a family of level-$\alpha$ tests, where $\delta_{\theta_0}$ tests $H_0: \theta = \theta_0$ vs. $H_1: \theta \neq \theta_0$. Define the random set $$\mathrm{CI}(\mathbf{X}) = \{\theta_0 \in \Theta : \delta_{\theta_0}(\mathbf{X}) = 0\},$$ i.e., the set of all parameter values that are not rejected by the data. Then $\mathrm{CI}(\mathbf{X})$ is a $(1-\alpha)$ confidence set: $$\mathbb{P}_\theta(\theta \in \mathrm{CI}(\mathbf{X})) = 1 - \alpha \quad \text{for all } \theta \in \Theta.$$

Theorem · CI–Test Duality

Let $[L(\mathbf{X}), U(\mathbf{X})]$ be a $(1-\alpha)$ confidence interval for $\theta$. Then the test that rejects $H_0: \theta = \theta_0$ if and only if $\theta_0 \notin [L, U]$ has size $\alpha$. Conversely, inverting a family of level-$\alpha$ tests produces a $(1-\alpha)$ confidence set.

Proof

$\mathbb{P}_\theta(\text{reject } H_0: \theta) = \mathbb{P}_\theta(\theta \notin [L,U]) = 1 - \mathbb{P}_\theta(\theta \in [L,U]) = 1 - (1-\alpha) = \alpha.$ $\blacksquare$

The practical consequence: if a $(1-\alpha)$ CI for $\theta$ excludes $\theta_0$, then a two-sided level-$\alpha$ test rejects $H_0: \theta = \theta_0$, and vice versa. This means you can test any specific value by simply checking whether it lies inside the confidence interval, without recomputing the test statistic from scratch.

Example · Using the CI Duality in Practice

Setup: A biostatistician at Oxford computes a 95% CI for a drug effect: $[0.12, 0.84]$ units.

  • Is the effect different from zero? Yes, because $0 \notin [0.12, 0.84]$. The two-sided test at $\alpha = 0.05$ rejects $H_0: \theta = 0$.
  • Is the effect greater than 0.5? No, because $0.5 \in [0.12, 0.84]$. The two-sided test at $\alpha = 0.05$ does not reject $H_0: \theta = 0.5$.
  • Is the effect greater than 1.0? Yes: $1.0 \notin [0.12, 0.84]$. The test rejects $H_0: \theta = 1.0$.

All three decisions are read directly from the CI without additional computation.

Intuition · The CI as a “Test for Every Value”

A confidence interval is literally the set of all null hypotheses you would fail to reject at level $\alpha$. Values inside the interval are “consistent with the data”; values outside are “rejected by the data.” This is why reporting a CI is more informative than reporting a p-value: the CI tells you both the range of plausible values and implicitly reveals all rejection decisions at once.

Warning · One-Sided vs. Two-Sided Duality

The duality between a $(1-\alpha)$ two-sided CI and a two-sided level-$\alpha$ test is exact. However, one-sided tests correspond to one-sided CIs (lower or upper confidence bounds). If you use a 95% CI (two-sided) to adjudicate a one-sided test at $\alpha = 0.05$, you are implicitly using $\alpha/2 = 0.025$ for that direction — a more conservative threshold than intended.

4.5 The z-Test for a Normal Mean

The z-test is the paradigmatic large-sample hypothesis test, applicable whenever the test statistic has an asymptotically normal distribution under the null. Its exact form applies when testing the mean of a population with known variance, which in practice often arises as a large-sample approximation.

4.5.1 One-Sample z-Test: Setup and Derivation

Model: $X_1, \ldots, X_n \overset{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim} N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ with $\sigma^2$ known. We test $$H_0: \mu = \mu_0 \quad \text{vs.} \quad H_1: \mu \neq \mu_0 \;(\text{or } > \mu_0, \text{ or } < \mu_0).$$

Sufficient statistic: By the factorization theorem, $\bar{X} = \frac{1}{n}\sum X_i$ is a sufficient statistic for $\mu$. Its distribution is $$\bar{X} \sim N\!\left(\mu, \frac{\sigma^2}{n}\right).$$

Standardization: Under $H_0: \mu = \mu_0$: $$Z = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu_0}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}} \sim N(0,1).$$ This pivotal quantity has a distribution entirely free of unknown parameters, enabling exact inference.

Definition · One-Sample z-Test

Given $X_1, \ldots, X_n \overset{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim} N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ with $\sigma^2$ known, and observed $\bar{x}$, the z-statistic is $$Z = \frac{\bar{x} - \mu_0}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}}.$$ The rejection regions and p-values for the three alternatives are:

Alternative $H_1$Reject $H_0$ ifp-value
$\mu > \mu_0$$Z > z_\alpha$$\Phi(-Z) = 1 - \Phi(Z)$
$\mu < \mu_0$$Z < -z_\alpha$$\Phi(Z)$
$\mu \neq \mu_0$$|Z| > z_{\alpha/2}$$2\Phi(-|Z|)$

where $z_\alpha = \Phi^{-1}(1-\alpha)$ is the upper-$\alpha$ quantile of $N(0,1)$.

4.5.2 Power of the z-Test

Theorem · Power Function of the One-Sided z-Test

For testing $H_0: \mu \leq \mu_0$ vs. $H_1: \mu > \mu_0$ at level $\alpha$, the power function is $$\pi(\mu) = \mathbb{P}_\mu(Z > z_\alpha) = \mathbb{P}_\mu\!\left(\frac{\bar{X} - \mu_0}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}} > z_\alpha\right) = \Phi\!\left(\frac{\mu - \mu_0}{\sigma/\sqrt{n}} - z_\alpha\right) = \Phi\!\left(\sqrt{n} \cdot \frac{\mu - \mu_0}{\sigma} - z_\alpha\right).$$ Properties:

  • At $\mu = \mu_0$: $\pi(\mu_0) = \Phi(-z_\alpha) = \alpha$ (test has correct size).
  • $\pi(\mu)$ is strictly increasing in $\mu$ (confirms UMP property).
  • $\pi(\mu) \to 1$ as $\mu \to \infty$ (consistent test).
  • $\pi(\mu) \to \alpha$ as $n \to 0$ (no data means no power beyond size).

Example · Power Calculation for a Clinical Trial (Columbia-Level)

Setup: A drug company believes a new medication reduces systolic blood pressure by $\delta = 5$ mmHg with population SD $\sigma = 15$ mmHg. They plan a one-sided test at $\alpha = 0.05$ with $n = 100$ patients.

Power: $$\pi = \Phi\!\left(\sqrt{100} \cdot \frac{5}{15} - 1.645\right) = \Phi\!\left(3.333 - 1.645\right) = \Phi(1.688) \approx 0.954.$$ The study has approximately 95.4% power to detect a 5 mmHg reduction — an excellent design.

Sample size for 80% power: Setting $\Phi(\delta\sqrt{n}/\sigma - z_\alpha) = 0.80$: $$n = \left(\frac{\sigma(z_\alpha + z_\beta)}{\delta}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{15(1.645 + 0.842)}{5}\right)^2 = \left(\frac{15 \times 2.487}{5}\right)^2 = 7.461^2 \approx 56.$$ Only 56 patients are needed to achieve 80% power in this setting.

4.5.3 Large-Sample z-Test

When $\sigma^2$ is unknown but $n$ is large, we replace $\sigma$ with the sample standard deviation $S = \sqrt{\frac{1}{n-1}\sum(X_i - \bar{X})^2}$. By Slutsky’s theorem and the CLT: $$Z = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu_0}{S/\sqrt{n}} \xrightarrow{d} N(0,1) \quad \text{as } n \to \infty.$$ This asymptotic result justifies using z-critical values for large $n$, even without the normality assumption.

Intuition · When is $n$ Large Enough for the z-Test?

The rule of thumb “$n \geq 30$” is rough; the required sample size depends on the distribution’s skewness. For symmetric distributions, $n = 15$–$20$ often suffices. For highly skewed distributions (e.g., income, claim sizes), you may need $n \geq 100$ or more. Alternatives: use the t-test (exact under normality), apply a normalizing transformation, or use bootstrap methods.

4.6 The t-Test: One-Sample and Two-Sample

The z-test requires a known population variance $\sigma^2$, which is rarely available in practice. The t-test, developed by William Sealy Gosset (writing as “Student”) in 1908, replaces $\sigma$ with the sample standard deviation $S$. The resulting test statistic follows a t-distribution under normality, providing exact inference for any sample size.

Figure 4.3. The standard normal (blue, solid) and $t$-distributions with $\nu = 3, 5, 30$ degrees of freedom. Heavy tails of the $t$ produce wider critical values; as $\nu \to \infty$ the $t_{\nu}$ converges to $N(0,1)$.

4.6.1 One-Sample t-Test

Model: $X_1, \ldots, X_n \overset{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim} N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ with both $\mu$ and $\sigma^2$ unknown.

Definition · One-Sample t-Test

The t-statistic is $$T = \frac{\bar{X} - \mu_0}{S/\sqrt{n}},$$ where $\bar{X} = \frac{1}{n}\sum X_i$ and $S^2 = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum(X_i - \bar{X})^2$.

Under $H_0: \mu = \mu_0$, by Cochran’s theorem: $$T \sim t_{n-1} \quad \text{(Student’s t-distribution with $n-1$ degrees of freedom).}$$ The p-values and rejection regions mirror the z-test but use $t_{n-1}$ quantiles.

Theorem · Pivotal Decomposition (Cochran)

If $X_1, \ldots, X_n \overset{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim} N(\mu, \sigma^2)$, then:

  1. $\bar{X}$ and $S^2$ are independent.
  2. $\frac{(n-1)S^2}{\sigma^2} \sim \chi^2_{n-1}$.
  3. Therefore $T = \frac{(\bar{X} - \mu)/\sigma_{\bar{X}}}{S/\sigma} = \frac{Z}{\sqrt{\chi^2_{n-1}/(n-1)}} \sim t_{n-1}$,
where the last expression uses the definition of the t-distribution as a ratio of an independent standard normal to the square root of a scaled chi-square.

Example · One-Sample t-Test (Oxford-Level Problem)

Setup: A materials scientist measures the tensile strength (MPa) of $n = 16$ steel specimens: $\bar{x} = 623.4$, $s = 14.2$. The specification requires mean strength $\mu_0 = 615$ MPa. Test $H_0: \mu = 615$ vs. $H_1: \mu > 615$ at $\alpha = 0.05$.

Test statistic: $$t = \frac{623.4 - 615}{14.2/\sqrt{16}} = \frac{8.4}{3.55} = 2.366.$$

Critical value: $t_{15, 0.05} = 1.753$ (upper 5th percentile of $t_{15}$).

Decision: Since $t = 2.366 > 1.753$, we reject $H_0$. The data provide significant evidence at $\alpha = 0.05$ that the mean strength exceeds 615 MPa.

p-value: $p = \mathbb{P}(t_{15} \geq 2.366) \approx 0.016$. The result is also significant at $\alpha = 0.02$ but not at $\alpha = 0.01$ (since $t_{15, 0.01} = 2.602$).

95% CI (one-sided lower bound): $\mu > \bar{x} - t_{15,0.05} \cdot s/\sqrt{n} = 623.4 - 1.753 \times 3.55 = 617.2$ MPa.

4.6.2 Two-Sample t-Test: Pooled (Equal Variances)

Model: Independent samples $X_1, \ldots, X_m \sim N(\mu_X, \sigma^2)$ and $Y_1, \ldots, Y_n \sim N(\mu_Y, \sigma^2)$ with common (unknown) variance $\sigma^2$. We test $H_0: \mu_X = \mu_Y$, i.e., $\Delta = \mu_X - \mu_Y = 0$.

Definition · Pooled Two-Sample t-Test

The pooled variance estimator combines both sample variances: $$S_p^2 = \frac{(m-1)S_X^2 + (n-1)S_Y^2}{m+n-2}.$$ Under $H_0$, $S_p^2$ is an unbiased estimator of $\sigma^2$ with $m+n-2$ degrees of freedom. The pooled t-statistic is $$T = \frac{\bar{X} - \bar{Y}}{S_p\sqrt{1/m + 1/n}} \sim t_{m+n-2} \quad \text{under } H_0.$$

4.6.3 Welch’s t-Test: Unequal Variances

When variances are unequal ($\sigma_X^2 \neq \sigma_Y^2$), the pooled t-test is invalid (it inflates Type I error when variances differ substantially). Welch’s t-test does not assume equal variances:

Definition · Welch’s t-Test

The Welch statistic is $$T_W = \frac{\bar{X} - \bar{Y}}{\sqrt{S_X^2/m + S_Y^2/n}}.$$ Under $H_0: \mu_X = \mu_Y$, $T_W$ is approximately $t_\nu$ where $\nu$ is given by the Welch–Satterthwaite formula: $$\nu = \frac{(S_X^2/m + S_Y^2/n)^2}{\frac{(S_X^2/m)^2}{m-1} + \frac{(S_Y^2/n)^2}{n-1}}.$$ The degrees of freedom $\nu$ are generally non-integer and always satisfy $\min(m-1, n-1) \leq \nu \leq m+n-2$.

Welch’s test is the default recommendation in most modern statistical packages (R uses it by default in t.test()) because it is robust to variance heterogeneity with minimal power loss when variances are actually equal.

4.6.4 Paired t-Test

When observations are collected in pairs — each pair consisting of two related measurements (before/after, matched subjects, or two conditions applied to the same unit) — the paired t-test exploits the correlation within pairs to gain power.

Definition · Paired t-Test

Given pairs $(X_i, Y_i)$, $i = 1, \ldots, n$, define the differences $D_i = X_i - Y_i$. If $D_i \overset{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim} N(\delta, \sigma_D^2)$, the test of $H_0: \delta = 0$ reduces to the one-sample t-test on $D_1, \ldots, D_n$: $$T = \frac{\bar{D}}{S_D/\sqrt{n}} \sim t_{n-1} \quad \text{under } H_0,$$ where $\bar{D} = \frac{1}{n}\sum D_i$ and $S_D^2 = \frac{1}{n-1}\sum(D_i - \bar{D})^2$.

Example · Paired vs. Independent t-Test — Why Pairing Matters

Setup: 12 patients have their blood pressure measured before and after a new drug. The differences (before $-$ after) are: $D = (3, -1, 5, 2, 8, 4, 6, 1, -2, 7, 5, 4)$. We test $H_0: \delta = 0$ vs. $H_1: \delta > 0$.

Calculation: $\bar{D} = 3.5$, $S_D = 2.97$, $n = 12$. $$t = \frac{3.5}{2.97/\sqrt{12}} = \frac{3.5}{0.857} = 4.08, \quad p = \mathbb{P}(t_{11} \geq 4.08) \approx 0.0009.$$ Strong evidence of a blood pressure reduction.

Why pairing helps: Between-patient variability in baseline blood pressure can be enormous, making a two-sample test powerless. Pairing subtracts this variability: $\sigma_D^2 = \sigma_X^2 + \sigma_Y^2 - 2\rho\sigma_X\sigma_Y$. When $\rho > 0$ (as is typical for repeated measures), $\sigma_D < \sqrt{\sigma_X^2 + \sigma_Y^2}$, giving higher power.

Data Science Application · t-Tests in Machine Learning Model Comparison

When comparing two classifiers on a common test set, practitioners often compute accuracy on $k$ cross-validation folds and apply a paired t-test on the $k$ accuracy differences. This is known as the Dietterich 5×2 cv t-test or simply the paired cross-validation t-test. Pairing eliminates fold-to-fold variability in dataset difficulty, greatly increasing sensitivity.

However, cross-validation folds are not truly independent (they share training data), so the t-distribution approximation is only approximate. McNemar’s test (Section 4.12) is preferred when the test set is fixed and binary predictions are available.

4.7 Comparing Two Normal Means: Structural Conditions

A fundamental choice in two-sample testing is whether to assume equal population variances (leading to the pooled t-test) or to allow them to differ (Welch’s test). This decision should be made based on scientific reasoning or formal pre-testing, not by examining which gives a smaller p-value. Post-hoc selection of the test inflates Type I error.

4.7.1 The Behrens–Fisher Problem

The exact solution to the problem of testing $H_0: \mu_X = \mu_Y$ when $\sigma_X^2 \neq \sigma_Y^2$ are both unknown is known as the Behrens–Fisher problem. No exact, uniformly most powerful test exists in this setting. This is one of the few classical hypothesis testing problems that lacks a clean exact solution for finite samples.

The Welch–Satterthwaite approximation solves the problem approximately: the statistic $T_W$ is approximately $t_\nu$ with $\nu$ estimated from the data. The approximation is extremely good in practice, and the coverage probability of Welch CIs exceeds the nominal level when variances differ substantially — unlike the pooled CI, which can have badly incorrect coverage.

Definition · Equal Variance Assumption — When is it Plausible?

The assumption $\sigma_X^2 = \sigma_Y^2$ is scientifically plausible when:

  • The two groups are treated exactly the same except for the experimental manipulation, and the manipulation is not expected to affect the variance.
  • The data are log-transformed and both groups have the same coefficient of variation on the original scale.
  • The study is a randomized controlled trial where assignment is random and both groups are drawn from the same population.

If the ratio $S_X^2 / S_Y^2$ exceeds 4 or falls below 0.25, it is generally unsafe to assume equal variances regardless of sample size.

4.7.2 Levene’s Test for Equality of Variances

Before applying the pooled t-test, one sometimes applies Levene’s test to check $H_0: \sigma_X^2 = \sigma_Y^2$. Levene’s statistic is based on the transformed deviations $Z_{ij} = |X_{ij} - \tilde{\mu}_i|$ where $\tilde{\mu}_i$ is the group mean or median:

$$W = \frac{(N-k)}{k-1} \cdot \frac{\sum_i n_i(\bar{Z}_{i\cdot} - \bar{Z}_{\cdot\cdot})^2}{\sum_i \sum_j (Z_{ij} - \bar{Z}_{i\cdot})^2} \sim F_{k-1, N-k} \quad \text{approximately under } H_0.$$
Warning · Pre-Testing for Variance Equality

The common strategy of first testing variance equality and then choosing between pooled and Welch t-tests (a “pre-test” procedure) is statistically problematic. It inflates the overall Type I error because the final test is no longer truly level $\alpha$. The modern recommendation is to use Welch’s test as the default, without pre-testing. Welch’s test is nearly as powerful as the pooled test when variances are equal, but maintains validity when they are not.

Example · Choosing Between Pooled and Welch (Decision Table)
ScenarioRecommended TestReason
RCT, normality holds, $n_1 \approx n_2$Pooled or Welch (similar)Balanced design is robust to variance heterogeneity
Observational, $\sigma_X \neq \sigma_Y$ suspectedWelchCorrect coverage regardless of variance ratio
Small $n$, heavy tailsMann–Whitney (Section 4.13)t-test assumptions may be violated
$n_1 / n_2 > 3$ or $< 1/3$WelchPooled t-test is sensitive to variance inequality in unbalanced designs

4.8 Tests for Variance: Chi-Square and F

In many scientific applications, the variance of a distribution is as important as its mean. Manufacturing processes must maintain tight tolerances; financial risk models focus on volatility. This section develops the classical tests for variance parameters.

Figure 4.5. Chi-square distributions $\chi^2_\nu$ for $\nu = 2$ (red), $5$ (gold), $10$ (blue), $20$ (green). As $\nu$ grows, the distribution shifts right and becomes more symmetric, approaching normality.

4.8.1 One-Sample Chi-Square Test for Variance

Model: $X_1, \ldots, X_n \overset{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim} N(\mu, \sigma^2)$ with $\mu$ unknown. We test $H_0: \sigma^2 = \sigma_0^2$.

Definition · One-Sample Chi-Square Test for Variance

Under $H_0: \sigma^2 = \sigma_0^2$, by Cochran’s theorem: $$Q = \frac{(n-1)S^2}{\sigma_0^2} \sim \chi^2_{n-1}.$$ Rejection regions at level $\alpha$:

  • Right-sided ($H_1: \sigma^2 > \sigma_0^2$): Reject if $Q > \chi^2_{n-1, 1-\alpha}$.
  • Left-sided ($H_1: \sigma^2 < \sigma_0^2$): Reject if $Q < \chi^2_{n-1, \alpha}$.
  • Two-sided ($H_1: \sigma^2 \neq \sigma_0^2$): Reject if $Q > \chi^2_{n-1, 1-\alpha/2}$ or $Q < \chi^2_{n-1, \alpha/2}$.
The two-sided test is not uniformly most powerful unbiased (UMPU) for this problem, but is the conventional choice.

Theorem · Distribution of $S^2$ Under Normality

If $X_1, \ldots, X_n \overset{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim} N(\mu, \sigma^2)$, then: $$\frac{(n-1)S^2}{\sigma^2} \sim \chi^2_{n-1}.$$ This follows from writing $S^2 = \frac{1}{n-1} \mathbf{X}_c^\top \mathbf{X}_c$ where $\mathbf{X}_c$ is the centered data, and noting that the projection onto the orthogonal complement of $\mathbf{1}/\sqrt{n}$ has rank $n-1$ and preserves independence from $\bar{X}$ by Cochran’s theorem.

Warning · Chi-Square Test Sensitivity to Non-Normality

The chi-square test for variance is highly sensitive to departures from normality — far more so than the t-test for the mean. Under non-normal distributions, the test may have drastically incorrect size. For example, if the true distribution is exponential, the chi-square test at nominal level $\alpha = 0.05$ may have an actual rejection rate of 20% or more. When normality is questionable, use robust variance tests (e.g., Levene’s, Brown–Forsythe) or bootstrap methods.

Example · Quality Control Variance Test (MIT-Level)

Setup: A precision parts manufacturer requires that the diameter variance of ball bearings not exceed $\sigma_0^2 = 0.0001$ mm$^2$ (i.e., $\sigma_0 = 0.01$ mm). A sample of $n = 25$ bearings yields $S^2 = 0.000152$ mm$^2$. Test $H_0: \sigma^2 \leq 0.0001$ vs. $H_1: \sigma^2 > 0.0001$ at $\alpha = 0.05$.

Test statistic: $$Q = \frac{24 \times 0.000152}{0.0001} = \frac{0.003648}{0.0001} = 36.48.$$

Critical value: $\chi^2_{24, 0.95} = 36.42$.

Decision: Since $Q = 36.48 > 36.42$, we marginally reject $H_0$ at $\alpha = 0.05$. The variance exceeds the specification limit, though barely. A larger sample would clarify the conclusion.

p-value: $p = \mathbb{P}(\chi^2_{24} \geq 36.48) \approx 0.048$.

4.8.2 F-Test for Equality of Two Variances

Figure 4.10. F-distributions $F_{d_1, d_2}$ for $(d_1, d_2) \in \{(2,5), (5,10), (10,30), (30,100)\}$. The distribution is right-skewed for small degrees of freedom and approaches normality as both $d_1, d_2 \to \infty$.

Model: Independent samples $X_1, \ldots, X_m \sim N(\mu_X, \sigma_X^2)$ and $Y_1, \ldots, Y_n \sim N(\mu_Y, \sigma_Y^2)$. We test $H_0: \sigma_X^2 = \sigma_Y^2$, equivalently $H_0: \sigma_X^2/\sigma_Y^2 = 1$.

Definition · F-Test for Variance Equality

The F-statistic is the ratio of sample variances: $$F = \frac{S_X^2}{S_Y^2}.$$ Under $H_0: \sigma_X^2 = \sigma_Y^2$, by the independence of $S_X^2$ and $S_Y^2$ and their chi-square distributions: $$F = \frac{S_X^2/\sigma_X^2}{S_Y^2/\sigma_Y^2} \cdot \frac{\sigma_X^2}{\sigma_Y^2} = \frac{\chi^2_{m-1}/(m-1)}{\chi^2_{n-1}/(n-1)} \sim F_{m-1, n-1}.$$ Rejection region for the two-sided test at level $\alpha$: $$F > F_{m-1, n-1, 1-\alpha/2} \quad \text{or} \quad F < F_{m-1, n-1, \alpha/2} = \frac{1}{F_{n-1, m-1, 1-\alpha/2}}.$$

The F-distribution has two parameters: numerator degrees of freedom $d_1 = m-1$ and denominator degrees of freedom $d_2 = n-1$. Conventionally, the larger sample variance is placed in the numerator so $F \geq 1$, which simplifies table lookup but requires adjusting the p-value (multiply by 2 for a two-sided test).

Example · F-Test in Two-Sample Comparison

Setup: Two production lines yield samples of size $m = 21$ and $n = 31$ with $S_X^2 = 18.4$ and $S_Y^2 = 9.2$. Test $H_0: \sigma_X^2 = \sigma_Y^2$ vs. $H_1: \sigma_X^2 \neq \sigma_Y^2$ at $\alpha = 0.10$.

F-statistic: $F = 18.4 / 9.2 = 2.0$, on $F_{20, 30}$.

Critical value: $F_{20, 30, 0.95} \approx 1.93$ (upper 5% of $F_{20,30}$).

Decision: Since $F = 2.0 > 1.93$, we reject $H_0$ at $\alpha = 0.10$. Evidence suggests the variances differ; use Welch’s test if subsequently comparing means.

Warning · F-Test Sensitivity to Non-Normality

Like the chi-square variance test, the F-test for equality of variances is very sensitive to non-normality. Under heavy-tailed distributions, the F-test can have severely inflated Type I error. Modern practice favors Levene’s test or Brown–Forsythe test, both of which are robust alternatives.

4.9 Tests for Proportions

Proportion tests are among the most widely used in applied statistics, arising in clinical trials (response rates), quality control (defect rates), and social science surveys. The population proportion $p$ plays the role of the unknown parameter, and the natural estimator is the sample proportion $\hat{p} = X/n$ where $X \sim \mathrm{Binomial}(n, p)$.

4.9.1 One-Sample Proportion Test

Setup: $X \sim \mathrm{Binomial}(n, p)$. Test $H_0: p = p_0$.

Exact test (Exact binomial): The exact p-value for the right-sided test is $\mathbb{P}(X \geq x_\mathrm{obs}; p_0)$, computed directly from the binomial CDF. This is exact under the model but can be conservative due to discreteness.

Large-sample z-test: By the CLT, $\hat{p} = X/n$ is approximately $N(p, p(1-p)/n)$. Under $H_0: p = p_0$: $$Z = \frac{\hat{p} - p_0}{\sqrt{p_0(1-p_0)/n}} \overset{\mathrm{approx}}{\sim} N(0,1).$$ The approximation is adequate when $np_0 \geq 5$ and $n(1-p_0) \geq 5$.

Example · One-Sample Proportion Test in Clinical Trials

Setup: Historical data shows a disease remission rate of $p_0 = 0.40$. A new therapy is applied to $n = 150$ patients; $x = 72$ achieve remission. Test $H_0: p = 0.40$ vs. $H_1: p > 0.40$ at $\alpha = 0.05$.

$\hat{p} = 72/150 = 0.480$.

$$Z = \frac{0.480 - 0.40}{\sqrt{0.40 \times 0.60 / 150}} = \frac{0.080}{0.04} = 2.00, \quad p = 1 - \Phi(2.00) = 0.023.$$

Reject $H_0$: the new therapy achieves a significantly higher remission rate at $\alpha = 0.05$.

4.9.2 Two-Sample Proportion Test

Setup: $X_1 \sim \mathrm{Bin}(n_1, p_1)$, $X_2 \sim \mathrm{Bin}(n_2, p_2)$, independent. Test $H_0: p_1 = p_2$.

Under $H_0$, both groups share a common proportion $p$, estimated by the pooled proportion: $$\hat{p} = \frac{X_1 + X_2}{n_1 + n_2}.$$ The test statistic is: $$Z = \frac{\hat{p}_1 - \hat{p}_2}{\sqrt{\hat{p}(1-\hat{p})(1/n_1 + 1/n_2)}} \overset{\mathrm{approx}}{\sim} N(0,1) \quad \text{under } H_0.$$

Intuition · Why Pool the Variance Under $H_0$?

Under $H_0: p_1 = p_2 = p$, the best estimate of the common $p$ uses all data: $\hat{p} = (X_1 + X_2)/(n_1 + n_2)$. Using separate estimates $\hat{p}_1$ and $\hat{p}_2$ in the denominator (as in the CI formula) is correct under $H_1$ but less efficient under $H_0$. Pooling is the standard choice for the test statistic; separate estimates are used for confidence intervals.

4.9.3 Fisher’s Exact Test

For small samples where the chi-square approximation is unreliable (expected cell counts below 5), Fisher’s exact test provides an exact p-value for testing independence in a $2 \times 2$ contingency table. Given fixed row and column margins, the count $X_1$ follows a hypergeometric distribution under $H_0: p_1 = p_2$: $$\mathbb{P}(X_1 = k \mid X_1 + X_2 = m) = \frac{\binom{n_1}{k}\binom{n_2}{m-k}}{\binom{n_1+n_2}{m}}.$$ The p-value sums hypergeometric probabilities over all tables at least as extreme as observed.

Example · Fisher’s Exact Test in Drug Safety

Setup: Nausea observed in $x_1 = 4$ of $n_1 = 20$ patients on a new drug vs. $x_2 = 0$ of $n_2 = 15$ on placebo. Test $H_0: p_1 = p_2$ at $\alpha = 0.05$ (two-sided).

Chi-square fails: $E_{11} = 20 \times 4/35 = 2.3 < 5$. Fisher’s exact p-value: $p = 0.087$. Fail to reject $H_0$ — not significant with these small samples, though the 95% CI $(-0.02, 0.40)$ shows a potentially substantial true effect consistent with the data.

4.9.4 Sample Size for Proportion Tests

For a one-sample test of $H_0: p = p_0$ vs. $H_1: p = p_1$ at level $\alpha$ and power $1-\beta$: $$n = \left\lceil \frac{(z_{\alpha/2}\sqrt{p_0(1-p_0)} + z_\beta\sqrt{p_1(1-p_1)})^2}{(p_1 - p_0)^2} \right\rceil.$$ This uses the variance under $H_0$ for the critical region and under $H_1$ for power, giving more accurate results than a simple approximation. For two-sample tests, use pooled variance under $H_0$ and summed variances under $H_1$.

Data Science Application · Proportion Tests in Quality Control

In manufacturing quality control, the proportion of defective items $p$ must be kept below a specification limit $p_0$. The test $H_0: p \leq p_0$ vs. $H_1: p > p_0$ is a one-sided proportion test. The acceptance sampling plan (sample size $n$, rejection number $c$) is derived from the power function of the exact binomial test: reject if $X > c$ where $X \sim \mathrm{Bin}(n, p)$. The OC curve (Operating Characteristic curve) plots the probability of acceptance as a function of the true defect rate $p$, enabling visualization of the trade-off between producer’s risk ($\alpha$, rejecting good lots) and consumer’s risk ($\beta$, accepting bad lots).

4.10 Tests for Poisson Parameters

Poisson models are ubiquitous for count data: hospital admission rates, software defect counts, radioactive decay events, network packet arrivals. Inference about the Poisson rate $\lambda$ proceeds through the sufficient statistic $T = \sum X_i$.

4.10.1 One-Sample Poisson Test

Setup: $X_1, \ldots, X_n \overset{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim} \mathrm{Poisson}(\lambda)$. Then $T = \sum X_i \sim \mathrm{Poisson}(n\lambda)$. Test $H_0: \lambda = \lambda_0$.

Exact test: The exact p-value is computed from the Poisson CDF with parameter $n\lambda_0$. For large $n$, the large-sample z-test applies: $$Z = \frac{\bar{X} - \lambda_0}{\sqrt{\lambda_0/n}} \approx N(0,1) \quad \text{under } H_0.$$

4.10.2 Two-Sample Poisson Rate Test

Setup: $X \sim \mathrm{Poisson}(\mu_1 t_1)$ and $Y \sim \mathrm{Poisson}(\mu_2 t_2)$ independently, where $\mu_1, \mu_2$ are the true rates and $t_1, t_2$ are the observation times. Test $H_0: \mu_1 = \mu_2$.

Conditional on $T = X + Y$, $X | T \sim \mathrm{Binomial}(T, p)$ where $p = t_1 \mu_1 / (t_1 \mu_1 + t_2 \mu_2)$. Under $H_0: \mu_1 = \mu_2$, $p_0 = t_1/(t_1 + t_2)$. This reduces to a one-sample proportion test on $X/T$.

4.10.3 Dispersion Test for Poisson Assumption

A key property of the Poisson distribution is equidispersion: $\mathbb{E}[X] = \mathrm{Var}(X) = \lambda$. Overdispersion (variance $>$ mean) or underdispersion (variance $<$ mean) indicates deviation from the Poisson model. The dispersion test statistic: $$D = \frac{(n-1)S^2}{\bar{X}} \approx \chi^2_{n-1} \quad \text{under Poisson.}$$ Large $D$ indicates overdispersion (negative binomial or mixture models may be needed); small $D$ indicates underdispersion (e.g., from thinning or inhibition processes).

Example · Poisson Rate Test for Hospital-Acquired Infections

Setup: A hospital records $x = 18$ hospital-acquired infections (HAIs) in $t_1 = 6$ months on a new protocol. Under the old protocol, the historical rate was $\lambda_0 = 4$ HAIs/month. Test $H_0: \lambda \geq 4$ vs. $H_1: \lambda < 4$ at $\alpha = 0.05$ (one-sided, right-sided rejection is for the old hypothesis).

Exact test: Under $H_0$, $X \sim \mathrm{Poisson}(n\lambda_0) = \mathrm{Poisson}(24)$. The p-value for the left-sided test: $$p = \mathbb{P}(\mathrm{Poisson}(24) \leq 18) \approx 0.105.$$ Fail to reject $H_0$ at $\alpha = 0.05$. The observed rate of 3 HAIs/month is consistent with the historical rate of 4 given the 6-month window; a longer observation period is needed to establish a significant reduction.

Large-sample z-test: $\bar{X} = 18/6 = 3.0$, $Z = (3.0 - 4)/\sqrt{4/6} = -1.0/0.816 = -1.22$, $p = \Phi(-1.22) \approx 0.111$. Close agreement with the exact test for this moderate rate.

4.10.4 Poisson Regression and Rate Adjustment

When comparing Poisson rates across groups with different exposure times or covariate profiles, the two-sample conditional test is insufficient. Poisson regression (a generalized linear model) models the log-rate as a linear function of covariates: $$\log(\lambda_i) = \log(t_i) + \mathbf{x}_i^\top \boldsymbol{\beta},$$ where $\log(t_i)$ is an offset that accounts for varying exposure times. Hypothesis tests on individual coefficients $\beta_j$ use the Wald test (asymptotically $N(0,1)$) or the likelihood ratio test (asymptotically $\chi^2_1$), both derived from the GLRT framework (Section 4.11).

Warning · Overdispersion in Poisson Models

Count data frequently exhibit overdispersion: the sample variance exceeds the sample mean, violating the Poisson equidispersion assumption. Causes include unobserved heterogeneity (individual-level random effects), clustering of events, or zero-inflation. Ignoring overdispersion leads to standard errors that are too small, resulting in artificially significant p-values. Remedies: Negative binomial regression (adds a dispersion parameter), quasi-Poisson (adjusts standard errors by the estimated dispersion factor), or zero-inflated models (ZIP, ZINB).

4.11 The Generalized Likelihood Ratio Test and Wilks’ Theorem

When the NP lemma does not apply — specifically when $H_0$ or $H_1$ is composite — we need a general-purpose method for constructing tests. The generalized likelihood ratio test (GLRT) provides a systematic approach that is asymptotically optimal in a precise sense.

4.11.1 The GLRT Statistic

Definition · Generalized Likelihood Ratio Test

Consider testing $H_0: \theta \in \Theta_0$ vs. $H_1: \theta \in \Theta_1 = \Theta \setminus \Theta_0$. Define:

  • $\hat{\theta}$: the unrestricted MLE (maximizing $L(\theta)$ over $\Theta$).
  • $\hat{\theta}_0$: the restricted MLE (maximizing $L(\theta)$ over $\Theta_0$).
  • The likelihood ratio statistic: $\Lambda = \dfrac{\sup_{\theta \in \Theta_0} L(\theta)}{\sup_{\theta \in \Theta} L(\theta)} = \dfrac{L(\hat{\theta}_0)}{L(\hat{\theta})} \in (0, 1].$
The GLRT rejects $H_0$ when $\Lambda$ is small, i.e., when $-2\log\Lambda$ is large: $$\mathrm{GLRT:} \quad \text{Reject } H_0 \text{ if } -2\log\Lambda > c_\alpha.$$

The quantity $-2\log\Lambda$ is non-negative (since $\Lambda \leq 1$, $\log \Lambda \leq 0$) and equals zero only if the restricted and unrestricted MLEs agree. It measures how much the likelihood decreases when we impose $H_0$: a small decrease (large $\Lambda$, small $-2\log\Lambda$) supports $H_0$; a large decrease (small $\Lambda$, large $-2\log\Lambda$) contradicts it.

4.11.2 Wilks’ Theorem

Theorem · Wilks’ Theorem

Let $\Theta \subseteq \mathbb{R}^p$ and $\Theta_0 = \{\theta : g_j(\theta) = 0, j = 1,\ldots,r\}$ impose $r$ independent smooth constraints ($r < p$). Under suitable regularity conditions and $H_0$: $$-2\log\Lambda \xrightarrow{d} \chi^2_r \quad \text{as } n \to \infty.$$ The degrees of freedom $r = \dim(\Theta) - \dim(\Theta_0)$ equals the number of constraints imposed by $H_0$.

Proof Sketch

Expand $\log L(\hat{\theta}_0)$ in a Taylor series around $\hat{\theta}$: $$\log L(\hat{\theta}_0) \approx \log L(\hat{\theta}) + (\hat{\theta}_0 - \hat{\theta})^\top \nabla \log L(\hat{\theta}) - \frac{1}{2}(\hat{\theta}_0 - \hat{\theta})^\top [- \nabla^2 \log L(\hat{\theta})](\hat{\theta}_0 - \hat{\theta}).$$ Since $\hat{\theta}$ is the MLE, $\nabla \log L(\hat{\theta}) = 0$. The Hessian $-\nabla^2 \log L(\hat{\theta}) / n \to I(\theta_0)$ (Fisher information), so: $$-2\log\Lambda \approx n(\hat{\theta}_0 - \hat{\theta})^\top I(\theta_0) (\hat{\theta}_0 - \hat{\theta}).$$ Under $H_0$, $\sqrt{n}(\hat{\theta} - \theta_0) \to N(0, I(\theta_0)^{-1})$ and the constraint forces $\hat{\theta}_0 - \hat{\theta}$ to lie in an $r$-dimensional subspace, yielding a $\chi^2_r$ limit. $\blacksquare$

Example · GLRT for the Normal Mean and Variance Jointly

Setup: $X_1, \ldots, X_n \overset{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim} N(\mu, \sigma^2)$, both unknown. Test $H_0: \mu = \mu_0$ (holding $\sigma^2$ free).

Restricted MLE: $\hat{\mu}_0 = \mu_0$, $\hat{\sigma}_0^2 = \frac{1}{n}\sum(X_i - \mu_0)^2$.

Unrestricted MLE: $\hat{\mu} = \bar{X}$, $\hat{\sigma}^2 = \frac{1}{n}\sum(X_i - \bar{X})^2 = \frac{n-1}{n}S^2$.

LR statistic: $$-2\log\Lambda = n\log\frac{\hat{\sigma}_0^2}{\hat{\sigma}^2} = n\log\!\left(1 + \frac{(\bar{X}-\mu_0)^2}{S^2/(n-1)} \cdot \frac{1}{n-1}\right).$$ By Wilks, $-2\log\Lambda \to \chi^2_1$ (one constraint: $\mu = \mu_0$). It can be shown that for the normal model, the GLRT is exactly equivalent to the t-test: $-2\log\Lambda = n\log(1 + T^2/(n-1))$, a monotone function of $T^2$. Thus the t-test is the GLRT for this problem.

Example · GLRT for Structural Break (Columbia-Level)

Problem: We observe a time series $Y_1, \ldots, Y_T$ and wish to test whether the mean changes at a known breakpoint $\tau$: $H_0: \mu_1 = \mu_2$ vs. $H_1: \mu_1 \neq \mu_2$, where $\mu_1$ is the pre-break mean and $\mu_2$ the post-break mean.

Under normality, this is a two-sample t-test. Under Wilks’ theorem, $-2\log\Lambda \to \chi^2_1$ (one constraint: $\mu_1 = \mu_2$). When $\tau$ is unknown, one searches over all candidate breakpoints and takes the maximum of the test statistics, requiring special asymptotic theory (Chow test, sup-Wald).

Data Science Application · Model Comparison via Likelihood Ratio

In regression modeling, the GLRT provides the standard method for comparing nested models. If Model 1 has $p$ parameters and Model 2 is a submodel with $q < p$ parameters (obtained by setting $r = p-q$ parameters to specific values), the LR statistic $-2(\ell_2 - \ell_1)$ is approximately $\chi^2_r$ under the null that the simpler model is correct. This is the basis for:

  • Likelihood ratio test in logistic regression: Testing whether additional predictors improve the model.
  • Deviance tests in GLMs: The deviance is $-2$ times the log-likelihood relative to a saturated model.
  • F-tests in ANOVA: Exact for linear models, approximations to the GLRT for other models.

4.12 Goodness-of-Fit Tests

Goodness-of-fit (GOF) tests assess whether a sample is consistent with a specified probability distribution. Unlike tests for specific parameters, GOF tests examine the entire distributional form. They are essential for model validation, assumption checking, and preliminary data analysis.

4.12.1 Pearson’s Chi-Square Goodness-of-Fit Test

Setup: Data consist of $n$ observations falling into $k$ mutually exclusive categories with observed counts $O_1, \ldots, O_k$ ($\sum O_i = n$) and hypothesized probabilities $p_1^{(0)}, \ldots, p_k^{(0)}$ ($\sum p_i^{(0)} = 1$). Expected counts are $E_i = n p_i^{(0)}$. Test $H_0: p_i = p_i^{(0)}$ for all $i$.

Definition · Pearson’s Chi-Square Statistic

$$X^2 = \sum_{i=1}^{k} \frac{(O_i - E_i)^2}{E_i}.$$ Under $H_0$, as $n \to \infty$: $X^2 \overset{d}{\to} \chi^2_{k-1}$ (one degree of freedom is lost because $\sum O_i = n$ is fixed). If $s$ parameters are estimated from the data (e.g., estimating $\lambda$ for a Poisson fit), the asymptotic distribution is $\chi^2_{k-1-s}$.

Theorem · Asymptotic Chi-Square Distribution

Under $H_0$ and as $n \to \infty$ with $E_i = np_i^{(0)} \to \infty$: $$X^2 = \sum_{i=1}^k \frac{(O_i - E_i)^2}{E_i} \xrightarrow{d} \chi^2_{k-1}.$$ Proof sketch: The vector $\mathbf{Z} = (O_1 - E_1, \ldots, O_k - E_k)/\sqrt{n}$ is asymptotically multivariate normal with singular covariance (because $\sum Z_i = 0$). The statistic $X^2/n = \mathbf{Z}^\top \mathrm{diag}(p_i^{(0)})^{-1} \mathbf{Z} / n$ is a quadratic form in $\mathbf{Z}$, and the singularity reduces the effective rank to $k-1$, giving $\chi^2_{k-1}$.

Warning · Minimum Expected Count Rule

The chi-square approximation requires $E_i \geq 5$ for all cells. When cells have small expected counts, merge adjacent categories or use Fisher’s exact test. A common violation: testing a distribution with many possible outcomes (e.g., Poisson) where the tail categories have very small expected counts.

4.12.2 Test of Independence in Contingency Tables

For two categorical variables $A$ (with $r$ levels) and $B$ (with $c$ levels), we form an $r \times c$ contingency table with cell counts $O_{ij}$. The hypothesis of independence is $H_0: p_{ij} = p_{i\cdot} p_{\cdot j}$ for all $(i,j)$.

Under $H_0$, expected counts are $E_{ij} = n \hat{p}_{i\cdot} \hat{p}_{\cdot j} = (R_i \cdot C_j)/n$ where $R_i = \sum_j O_{ij}$ are row totals and $C_j = \sum_i O_{ij}$ are column totals. The test statistic: $$X^2 = \sum_{i,j} \frac{(O_{ij} - E_{ij})^2}{E_{ij}} \xrightarrow{d} \chi^2_{(r-1)(c-1)}.$$ The degrees of freedom $(r-1)(c-1)$ account for the $(r-1) + (c-1) = r+c-2$ estimated marginal probabilities.

4.12.3 The G-Test (Likelihood Ratio GOF Test)

The G-test (also called the likelihood ratio test for goodness-of-fit) is the GLRT counterpart to Pearson’s chi-square: $$G = 2\sum_{i=1}^{k} O_i \ln\!\left(\frac{O_i}{E_i}\right).$$ Under $H_0$, $G \overset{d}{\to} \chi^2_{k-1}$ with the same degrees of freedom as the chi-square test. The G-test is additive (interactions can be decomposed naturally) and is the preferred test for log-linear models. In practice, $X^2$ and $G$ give nearly identical results for moderate to large samples.

Example · Chi-Square Test for a Genetic Hypothesis (Harvard Genetics)

Setup: Mendelian genetics predicts a 9:3:3:1 ratio for a two-gene cross. An observed sample of $n = 160$ yields counts (90, 30, 25, 15). Test $H_0:$ the 9:3:3:1 ratio holds.

Expected counts: $E = (160 \times 9/16, 160 \times 3/16, 160 \times 3/16, 160 \times 1/16) = (90, 30, 30, 10)$.

$$X^2 = \frac{(90-90)^2}{90} + \frac{(30-30)^2}{30} + \frac{(25-30)^2}{30} + \frac{(15-10)^2}{10} = 0 + 0 + 0.833 + 2.500 = 3.333.$$

Decision: $\chi^2_{3, 0.95} = 7.815$. Since $3.333 < 7.815$, fail to reject $H_0$. The data are consistent with the 9:3:3:1 ratio.

4.13 Non-Parametric Tests

Parametric tests (z, t, F, chi-square) assume the data follow a specific distributional family. When these assumptions are seriously violated — particularly for small samples from non-normal populations — non-parametric tests provide valid inference without distributional assumptions. They operate on ranks rather than raw values, achieving robustness at some cost in efficiency when normality holds.

4.13.1 The Mann–Whitney U Test

The Mann–Whitney U test (also called the Wilcoxon rank-sum test) is the non-parametric counterpart to the two-sample t-test. It tests whether one distribution tends to produce larger values than the other, without assuming normality or equal variances.

Definition · Mann–Whitney U Statistic

Given independent samples $X_1, \ldots, X_m$ and $Y_1, \ldots, Y_n$, the U statistic is: $$U = \sum_{i=1}^{m} \sum_{j=1}^{n} \mathbf{1}[X_i > Y_j].$$ $U$ counts the number of $(X_i, Y_j)$ pairs in which $X_i$ exceeds $Y_j$. By symmetry, $U + U' = mn$ where $U' = \sum_{i,j}\mathbf{1}[Y_j > X_i]$, so $U$ ranges from 0 to $mn$.

Equivalently, rank all $m+n$ observations jointly and let $W = \sum_{i=1}^m R_i$ be the sum of ranks of the $X$ sample. Then $U = W - m(m+1)/2$.

Null hypothesis: $H_0: P(X > Y) = 1/2$, i.e., the two distributions are identical (stochastic equality). Under $H_0$, all $\binom{m+n}{m}$ possible rank assignments are equally likely, giving: $$\mathbb{E}_0[U] = \frac{mn}{2}, \quad \mathrm{Var}_0(U) = \frac{mn(m+n+1)}{12}.$$ For large samples, $Z = (U - mn/2) / \sqrt{mn(m+n+1)/12} \approx N(0,1)$ under $H_0$.

Theorem · Asymptotic Normality of the U Statistic

The U statistic is a two-sample U-statistic with kernel $h(x,y) = \mathbf{1}[x > y]$. By Hoeffding’s (1948) theory of U-statistics: $$\sqrt{mn/(m+n)} \cdot (\hat\theta - \theta) \xrightarrow{d} N(0, \sigma^2_h)$$ where $\hat\theta = U/(mn)$ estimates $\theta = P(X > Y)$ and $\sigma^2_h$ involves the conditional variances of the kernel. This ensures consistent estimation and asymptotic normality for testing $H_0: \theta = 1/2$.

Example · Mann–Whitney Test for Pain Scores

Setup: A pain relief study compares a new drug ($m = 8$) vs. placebo ($n = 8$). Pain reduction scores: Drug: $\{4, 7, 3, 8, 5, 9, 6, 7\}$; Placebo: $\{2, 1, 4, 3, 5, 2, 3, 4\}$. Test at $\alpha = 0.05$ (two-sided).

Ranks: Assign ranks 1–16 to all 16 observations. Sum of drug ranks: $W = 89$. $$U = 89 - \frac{8 \times 9}{2} = 89 - 36 = 53.$$

Null distribution: $\mu_U = 32$, $\sigma_U = \sqrt{8 \times 8 \times 17/12} = 9.52$. $$Z = \frac{53 - 32}{9.52} = 2.21, \quad p = 2(1-\Phi(2.21)) \approx 0.027.$$ Reject $H_0$: the drug provides significantly greater pain relief at $\alpha = 0.05$.

4.13.2 The Wilcoxon Signed-Rank Test

The Wilcoxon signed-rank test is the non-parametric analogue of the one-sample or paired t-test. It tests whether the median of a symmetric distribution equals zero (or whether the median of differences in paired data equals zero). It uses both the sign and the magnitude of differences, making it more powerful than the sign test.

Definition · Wilcoxon Signed-Rank Statistic

Given $D_1, \ldots, D_n$ (differences from a symmetric, continuous distribution), let $|D_i|$ be the absolute values and $R_i^+$ their ranks (after discarding zeros). Define: $$W^+ = \sum_{i: D_i > 0} R_i^+.$$ Under $H_0: \text{median} = 0$ (and symmetry): $$\mathbb{E}_0[W^+] = \frac{n(n+1)}{4}, \quad \mathrm{Var}_0(W^+) = \frac{n(n+1)(2n+1)}{24}.$$ For large $n$, $Z = (W^+ - n(n+1)/4) / \sqrt{n(n+1)(2n+1)/24} \approx N(0,1)$ under $H_0$.

4.13.3 Asymptotic Relative Efficiency

Non-parametric tests are less powerful than their parametric counterparts when the parametric assumptions hold. The asymptotic relative efficiency (ARE) quantifies this trade-off: it is the limiting ratio of sample sizes required to achieve the same power and significance level as $n \to \infty$.

Key ARE values under normality:

  • Mann–Whitney vs. two-sample t: $\mathrm{ARE} = 3/\pi \approx 0.955$. The MW test needs only 4.8% more observations than the t-test to achieve the same power under normality.
  • Wilcoxon signed-rank vs. one-sample t: $\mathrm{ARE} = 3/\pi \approx 0.955$.
  • Sign test vs. t-test: $\mathrm{ARE} = 2/\pi \approx 0.637$. The sign test is substantially less efficient under normality.

Under heavy-tailed distributions (e.g., $t_3$, Cauchy), the ARE of the MW test vs. the t-test can exceed 1 — the non-parametric test becomes more efficient. For the Cauchy distribution, the ARE is infinite: the t-test is entirely non-convergent, while the MW test retains consistency and asymptotic normality. This is because the t-test is dominated by outliers while rank-based tests are not.

Data Science Application · When to Use Non-Parametric Tests

Practical guide to parametric vs. non-parametric choice:

  • Use t-test: Large $n$ ($> 30$ per group), continuous outcome on a meaningful scale, or confirmed normality.
  • Use Mann–Whitney: Ordinal outcomes (Likert scales, rankings), small $n$ with suspected non-normality, or when outliers are present and the median is the relevant quantity.
  • Use sign test: When only direction of differences matters, or when the signed-rank symmetry assumption is not met.
  • Use permutation test: Complex designs where no standard distribution theory applies; computes the exact p-value by enumerating or sampling the permutation distribution of the test statistic.

For the millions-of-rows datasets typical in production data science, the CLT guarantees t-test validity regardless of the underlying distribution, so non-parametric tests are rarely necessary. They remain important for clinical and social science settings with small samples.

4.14 Power Analysis and Sample Size Determination

One of the most important applications of hypothesis testing theory is determining how large a study needs to be before collecting data. Underpowered studies waste resources and fail to detect true effects; overpowered studies waste resources and may achieve statistical significance for trivially small effects. Prospective power analysis is the responsible approach to study design.

4.14.1 The Four Ingredients of Power Analysis

Power analysis involves four quantities, and knowing any three determines the fourth:

  • $\alpha$: the significance level (pre-specified Type I error rate, e.g., 0.05).
  • Power $= 1-\beta$: the desired probability of detecting a true effect (e.g., 0.80, 0.90).
  • Effect size $\delta$: the minimum scientifically meaningful difference to detect.
  • Sample size $n$: the number of observations per group.
Figure 4.9. Power of the one-sample right-sided z-test as a function of sample size $n$ for effect sizes $\\delta \\in \\{0.2, 0.5, 0.8\\}$ ($\\sigma = 1$, $\\alpha = 0.05$). Larger effect sizes require smaller samples to achieve 80% power.

4.14.2 Sample Size Formula for the One-Sample z-Test

Theorem · Sample Size for One-Sample z-Test

To test $H_0: \mu = \mu_0$ vs. $H_1: \mu = \mu_0 + \delta$ ($\delta > 0$, right-sided) with known $\sigma$, at level $\alpha$ and power $1-\beta$, the required sample size is: $$n = \left\lceil \frac{\sigma^2 (z_\alpha + z_\beta)^2}{\delta^2} \right\rceil.$$ For two-sided tests, replace $z_\alpha$ by $z_{\alpha/2}$: $$n = \left\lceil \frac{\sigma^2 (z_{\alpha/2} + z_\beta)^2}{\delta^2} \right\rceil.$$

Derivation

We need $\pi(\mu_0 + \delta) = 1 - \beta$: $$\Phi\!\left(\frac{\delta\sqrt{n}}{\sigma} - z_\alpha\right) = 1-\beta.$$ Taking $\Phi^{-1}$ of both sides: $\delta\sqrt{n}/\sigma - z_\alpha = z_\beta$, so $\sqrt{n} = \sigma(z_\alpha + z_\beta)/\delta$, giving $n = \sigma^2(z_\alpha + z_\beta)^2/\delta^2$. $\blacksquare$

Example · Sample Size Planning for an A/B Test

Setup: An e-commerce company runs a two-sided test on conversion rate. Baseline rate $p_0 = 0.05$ (5%), minimum detectable effect $\delta = 0.01$ (a 1 pp lift to 6%). Parameters: $\alpha = 0.05$, power $= 0.80$.

Normal approximation: Variance under $H_0$ is $p_0(1-p_0) = 0.0475$, so $\sigma \approx 0.218$. $$n = \frac{0.0475(1.96 + 0.842)^2}{(0.01)^2} = \frac{0.0475 \times 7.854}{0.0001} \approx 3{,}731 \text{ per group.}$$ Total: $7{,}462$ users. This illustrates why A/B tests on low-conversion outcomes need enormous samples — small absolute effects require large $n$ to detect.

4.14.3 Standardized Effect Sizes

The effect size $\delta$ should be specified in scientific terms before any analysis. Standardized effect sizes facilitate comparison across studies with different units:

Definition · Cohen’s Standardized Effect Sizes
  • Cohen’s $d$ (mean difference): $d = (\mu_1 - \mu_0)/\sigma$. Benchmarks: small $|d| = 0.2$, medium $|d| = 0.5$, large $|d| = 0.8$.
  • Cohen’s $h$ (proportions): $h = 2\arcsin(\sqrt{p_1}) - 2\arcsin(\sqrt{p_0})$, the variance-stabilizing transformation for the arcsine.
  • Cohen’s $f^2$ (regression): $f^2 = R^2/(1-R^2)$, measuring variance explained relative to unexplained variance.
  • Cohen’s $w$ (chi-square): $w = \sqrt{\chi^2/n}$, the root-mean-square effect for categorical outcomes.

4.14.4 Consequences of Underpowered Studies

Warning · The Underpowered Study Problem and Publication Bias

Published studies are subject to publication bias: statistically significant results are far more likely to be published. In a literature where typical power is 20–50% (common in psychology, neuroscience, and early genomics), two pathologies arise:

  • Winner’s curse: Only the most extreme sampling realizations of a true effect reach significance, so published effects are overestimates. A study with 30% power and a true effect will, on average, overestimate the effect by a factor of 1.5–2.0 among significant results.
  • False discovery inflation: If the prior probability that $H_1$ is true is $\pi_1 = 0.10$, power is 20%, and $\alpha = 0.05$, then the positive predictive value (PPV) of a significant result is: $$\mathrm{PPV} = \frac{0.20 \times 0.10}{0.20 \times 0.10 + 0.05 \times 0.90} = \frac{0.020}{0.065} \approx 31\%.$$ Most “significant” findings are false positives in this regime.

Remedy: Pre-register studies with power analysis. Require replication. Report CIs alongside p-values. Use meta-analysis to synthesize evidence across studies.

4.14.5 Sample Size for Two-Sample Comparisons

For comparing two independent group means with equal samples of size $n$ per group and common SD $\sigma$: $$n = \left\lceil \frac{2\sigma^2 (z_{\alpha/2} + z_\beta)^2}{\delta^2} \right\rceil \quad \text{(two-sided)},$$ where $\delta = |\mu_1 - \mu_2|$ is the MDE. For unequal allocation with $n_1 = r \cdot n_2$: $$n_2 = \left\lceil \frac{\sigma^2 (1 + 1/r)(z_{\alpha/2} + z_\beta)^2}{\delta^2} \right\rceil.$$ Balanced designs ($r = 1$) minimize the total sample size $n_1 + n_2$ for given power.

4.15 Multiple Testing: FWER and FDR Control

Modern science routinely tests thousands or millions of hypotheses simultaneously: genomics studies test tens of thousands of genes; brain imaging studies test hundreds of thousands of voxels; online platforms run hundreds of A/B tests concurrently. Testing each hypothesis at level $\alpha = 0.05$ without adjustment guarantees many false positives — even if no effect exists anywhere, 5% of tests will be significant by chance. Controlling the error rate across many tests requires specialized procedures.

Figure 4.6. Sorted p-values (blue circles) from $m = 50$ tests plotted against the Benjamini–Hochberg threshold $k\\alpha/m$ (red line) and Bonferroni threshold $\\alpha/m$ (gold dashed line). Rejected hypotheses (below the BH threshold) are highlighted in red.

4.15.1 The Family-Wise Error Rate

Definition · Family-Wise Error Rate (FWER)

Given $m$ hypothesis tests, the family-wise error rate is: $$\mathrm{FWER} = \mathbb{P}(\text{at least one false rejection}) = \mathbb{P}\!\left(\bigcup_{i \in \mathcal{H}_0} \{\delta_i = 1\}\right),$$ where $\mathcal{H}_0$ is the set of true null hypotheses. An FWER-controlling procedure at level $\alpha$ ensures $\mathrm{FWER} \leq \alpha$ under any configuration of true and false nulls.

Why naive testing fails: If $m = 100$ independent hypotheses are all truly null and each is tested at $\alpha = 0.05$: $$\mathrm{FWER} = 1 - (1-0.05)^{100} \approx 0.994.$$ A false positive is virtually certain.

4.15.2 Bonferroni Correction

Theorem · Bonferroni’s Inequality

By the union bound: $\mathbb{P}\!\left(\bigcup_{i=1}^m A_i\right) \leq \sum_{i=1}^m \mathbb{P}(A_i).$ Applying this with $A_i = \{\text{reject true } H_{0,i}\}$: if each test uses threshold $\alpha/m$, then $$\mathrm{FWER} \leq |\mathcal{H}_0| \cdot \frac{\alpha}{m} \leq m \cdot \frac{\alpha}{m} = \alpha.$$

Definition · Bonferroni Correction

Reject $H_{0,i}$ if $p_i \leq \alpha/m$. Adjusted p-values: $\tilde{p}_i = \min(m \cdot p_i, 1)$. The Bonferroni correction is valid under arbitrary dependence between tests.

The Bonferroni correction is simple and universally valid. Its weakness: it is conservative when tests are positively correlated (as in genomics). For $m = 10{,}000$ tests at $\alpha = 0.05$, the threshold is $5 \times 10^{-6}$ — stringent enough to miss many true effects.

4.15.3 Holm’s Step-Down Procedure

Definition · Holm’s Step-Down Procedure

Sort p-values: $p_{(1)} \leq p_{(2)} \leq \cdots \leq p_{(m)}$. Let $k = \min\{i : p_{(i)} > \alpha/(m-i+1)\}$. Reject $H_{0,(1)}, \ldots, H_{0,(k-1)}$; retain the rest. Holm’s procedure controls FWER at level $\alpha$ under any dependence and is uniformly more powerful than Bonferroni.

The key insight: once the least-significant hypothesis is retained, all subsequent (more extreme) hypotheses can be tested at progressively relaxed thresholds $\alpha/(m-1), \alpha/(m-2), \ldots, \alpha/1$.

4.15.4 The False Discovery Rate

In large-scale testing, controlling FWER is too conservative — it would require p-value thresholds below $10^{-7}$ in GWAS studies and miss virtually all true effects. The False Discovery Rate (FDR), introduced by Benjamini and Hochberg (1995), controls a more permissive criterion that allows some false positives while keeping their rate under control.

Definition · False Discovery Rate (FDR)

Let $V$ = number of false rejections (false positives) and $R$ = total rejections. The FDR is: $$\mathrm{FDR} = \mathbb{E}\!\left[\frac{V}{\max(R,1)}\right].$$ When all nulls are true ($m_0 = m$), $\mathrm{FDR} = \mathrm{FWER}$. When some nulls are false ($m_0 < m$), $\mathrm{FDR} < \mathrm{FWER}$: FDR control is less stringent than FWER control.

Theorem · Benjamini–Hochberg (BH) Procedure

Sort p-values $p_{(1)} \leq \cdots \leq p_{(m)}$. Let $\hat{k} = \max\{i : p_{(i)} \leq i\alpha/m\}$. Reject $H_{0,(1)}, \ldots, H_{0,(\hat{k})}$. Under independence (or PRDS): $$\mathrm{FDR} \leq \frac{m_0}{m} \cdot \alpha \leq \alpha,$$ where $m_0$ is the number of true nulls. Under arbitrary dependence, the BH procedure with threshold $\alpha/(1 + 1/2 + \cdots + 1/m)$ (the Benjamini–Yekutieli procedure) controls FDR at level $\alpha$.

Example · Applying BH in Genomics (Stanford Bioinformatics)

Setup: A differential expression study tests $m = 10{,}000$ genes at $\alpha = 0.05$. After sorting p-values, the largest $\hat{k}$ satisfying $p_{(k)} \leq k \times 5 \times 10^{-6}$ is $\hat{k} = 847$.

Interpretation: We reject 847 hypotheses. Expected false positives: $\leq 0.05 \times 847 \approx 42$. Compare to Bonferroni: rejects only genes with $p < 5 \times 10^{-6}$, likely far fewer. The BH procedure offers dramatically more power at the cost of tolerating a small fraction of false positives.

Storey’s q-value: The BH threshold is conservative when $m_0 \ll m$. Storey (2002) estimates $m_0/m$ directly from the data and uses the estimated $\pi_0 = m_0/m$ to compute calibrated q-values: $q_{(i)} = \pi_0 \cdot m \cdot p_{(i)} / i$. Rejecting hypotheses with $q_{(i)} \leq \alpha$ controls FDR at exactly level $\pi_0 \alpha \leq \alpha$.

Data Science Application · Multiple Testing in Online Experiments

Large tech companies face multiple testing challenges in two distinct settings:

  • Multiple metrics per experiment: A single A/B test may evaluate click rate, conversion, revenue, and session time simultaneously. Reporting the minimum p-value across $k$ metrics inflates Type I error by a factor of up to $k$. Standard fix: Bonferroni or Holm correction within each experiment.
  • Peeking at sequential results: Stopping an experiment early when $p < 0.05$ is first observed inflates FWER dramatically — it can reach 30% or higher for experiments stopped any time in the first 100% of planned observations. Sequential testing methods (Lan-DeMets alpha-spending, always-valid p-values, e-values) control error while permitting early stopping.

A growing practice: the “experimentation platform” at companies like Netflix, Airbnb, and LinkedIn implements multiple testing corrections automatically, with pre-specified primary metrics and secondary metrics analyzed under different correction regimes.

4.16 Graphical Diagnostics: Rootograms and Q-Q Plots

Formal goodness-of-fit tests compress a rich distributional comparison into a single scalar statistic, discarding information about the nature and location of departures from the model. Graphical diagnostics complement formal tests by revealing where and how a model fits or misfits the data. Two of the most powerful tools are the rootogram (for count distributions) and the Q-Q plot (for continuous distributions).

4.16.1 The Hanging Rootogram

Figure 4.8. Hanging rootogram for fitting a Poisson($\\lambda = 3.27$) distribution to count data. Bars extending below the baseline indicate underfitting; bars above indicate overfitting.

A rootogram displays the goodness-of-fit for count distributions by comparing observed and expected counts under a fitted model. The hanging rootogram (Tukey, 1972) plots differences on the square-root scale, which stabilizes variance and clarifies the pattern of misfits.

Definition · Hanging Rootogram

For count data with observed counts $O_k$ and fitted counts $E_k$, the hanging rootogram displays:

  • A reference curve at height $\sqrt{E_k}$ (the model prediction on the square-root scale).
  • Bars of height $\sqrt{O_k}$ hanging down from the reference curve; the bottom of each bar is at $\sqrt{E_k} - \sqrt{O_k}$.
