  • Bars extending below the baseline (negative gap) indicate underfitting: fewer observations than expected.
  • Bars remaining above the baseline indicate overfitting: more observations than expected.

The square-root transformation is the variance-stabilizing transformation for Poisson counts: $\mathrm{Var}(\sqrt{O_k}) \approx 1/4$, so all bars have approximately equal visual weight regardless of count magnitude.

Diagnosing model misfit with rootograms:

  • Perfect fit: All bars rest at or near the baseline. The rootogram is flat.
  • Overdispersion: In a Poisson fit, bars in the tails are above the baseline (more extreme counts than expected) while bars at moderate values are below; the variance exceeds the mean. Remedy: Negative binomial model.
  • Zero-inflation: The bar for count $k = 0$ is prominently above the baseline while the bar for $k = 1$ or $k = 2$ may be below. Remedy: Zero-inflated Poisson (ZIP) or hurdle model.
  • Underdispersion: Bars at moderate values above baseline, tails below; the variance is smaller than the mean. Remedy: Conway-Maxwell-Poisson or generalized Poisson model.

4.16.2 Quantile-Quantile (Q-Q) Plots

Figure 4.7. Normal Q-Q plots for $n = 200$ observations from a standard normal (left) and a heavy-tailed $t_3$ distribution (right). The normal sample aligns with the diagonal reference line; the $t_3$ sample shows characteristic S-shape deviations at the tails.

A Q-Q plot compares the quantile function of the empirical distribution to the quantile function of a reference distribution. Perfect agreement between data and model manifests as alignment with the 45-degree diagonal line.

Definition · Normal Q-Q Plot

Given order statistics $X_{(1)} \leq \cdots \leq X_{(n)}$, the normal Q-Q plot displays: $$\left(\Phi^{-1}\!\left(\frac{i-0.5}{n}\right),\; X_{(i)}\right) \quad i = 1,\ldots,n,$$ using the Hazen plotting position $(i-0.5)/n$. A reference line is drawn through the first and third quartiles (the interquartile line). If data are normally distributed, the plotted points cluster tightly around this reference line.

Interpreting Q-Q plot deviations:

  • Points above line in both tails (S-curve opening upward): Heavy tails (e.g., $t$, Cauchy, or other leptokurtic distributions). The observed quantiles in the tails are more extreme than normal quantiles.
  • Points below line in both tails (S-curve opening downward): Light tails (e.g., uniform, truncated distributions). The observed extremes are less extreme than normal.
  • Points above on right, below on left: Right skewness — a long upper tail.
  • Points below on right, above on left: Left skewness — a long lower tail.
  • Discrete steps: Discrete data, rounding, or grouping artifacts.
Example · Diagnosing Residual Non-Normality in Regression

Case 1 (Heavy tails): Residuals from a financial returns model produce a Q-Q plot with an S-curve: points curve upward in the upper right and downward in the lower left. This is the signature of heavier-than-normal tails. Action: Use a $t$-distribution error model, apply robust regression, or use heteroskedasticity-consistent standard errors.

Case 2 (Right skewness): Residuals from a model of income data show points hugging the diagonal for negative values but pulling sharply above the line for large positive values. Action: Apply a log or Box-Cox transformation to the response, or use a generalized linear model with a gamma or lognormal error distribution.

Case 3 (Single outlier): The Q-Q plot shows near-perfect alignment except for one point far above the line in the upper right. Action: Investigate the outlying observation. Check for data entry errors, influential observations (leverage, Cook’s distance), and consider its impact on inference.

4.16.3 P-P Plots and Complementary Diagnostics

A P-P plot plots the empirical CDF $F_n(x)$ against the theoretical CDF $F_0(x)$ at each order statistic. P-P plots are sensitive to deviations in the center of the distribution; Q-Q plots are more sensitive to deviations in the tails. For assessing normality as a prerequisite for t-tests, Q-Q plots are generally preferred because tail behavior is what matters most for the validity of the normal approximation.

Complementary diagnostics include:

  • Histogram with fitted density overlay: Shows the overall shape and any multimodality.
  • Box plot: Identifies outliers and skewness at a glance.
  • Empirical CDF plot: Compares the step function $F_n$ to the theoretical CDF $F_0$; the vertical gap is the KS statistic.
  • Worm plots (van Buuren, 2001): Detrended Q-Q plots that remove the reference line, making it easier to spot systematic patterns in the deviations.

4.17 Formal Normality Tests

While Q-Q plots provide qualitative assessment of normality, formal normality tests provide a quantitative decision. These tests carry the null hypothesis $H_0$: “the data are normally distributed” and $H_1$: “the data are not normally distributed.” Their purpose is not to prove normality (we can never prove a null) but to detect serious departures that would invalidate subsequent inference.

4.17.1 The Shapiro–Wilk Test

Definition · Shapiro–Wilk Test

Given order statistics $X_{(1)} \leq \cdots \leq X_{(n)}$, the Shapiro–Wilk statistic is: $$W = \frac{\left(\sum_{i=1}^{n} a_i X_{(i)}\right)^2}{\sum_{i=1}^{n} (X_i - \bar{X})^2},$$ where $a_i$ are coefficients derived from the expected values and covariance matrix of standard normal order statistics: $$\mathbf{a}^\top = \frac{\mathbf{m}^\top V^{-1}}{(\mathbf{m}^\top V^{-1} V^{-1} \mathbf{m})^{1/2}},$$ with $m_i = \mathbb{E}[Z_{(i)}]$ and $V_{ij} = \mathrm{Cov}(Z_{(i)}, Z_{(j)})$ for standard normal order statistics.

$W \in (0, 1]$, equaling 1 only if the sample is perfectly normal. Small $W$ indicates departure from normality. P-values are obtained from the Royston (1992) approximation, valid for $n \leq 5000$.

The Shapiro–Wilk test is widely regarded as the most powerful omnibus normality test, particularly for $n < 50$. The statistic $W$ is essentially the squared correlation between the order statistics and their expected values under normality — it measures how well the data follows the straight-line pattern in a Q-Q plot.

4.17.2 Kolmogorov–Smirnov and Lilliefors Tests

The Kolmogorov–Smirnov (KS) test measures the maximum absolute deviation between the empirical CDF $F_n(x)$ and the hypothesized CDF $F_0(x)$: $$D_n = \sup_x |F_n(x) - F_0(x)|.$$ Under $H_0$, the distribution of $D_n$ is distribution-free for any continuous $F_0$, enabling tabulation of universal critical values. The KS test is consistent (power $\to 1$ for any fixed alternative) and is valid under exact knowledge of $F_0$.

Critical limitation: If parameters of $F_0$ are estimated from the data, the KS p-value is anti-conservative (too many rejections). The Lilliefors test corrects this by simulating the distribution of $D_n$ when $\mu$ and $\sigma$ are estimated via MLE, providing accurate p-values for normality testing. Lilliefors p-values are substantially larger than raw KS p-values on the same data.

4.17.3 Anderson–Darling Test

The Anderson–Darling (AD) test weights the KS statistic more heavily in the tails, where departures from normality are most consequential for parametric inference: $$A^2 = -n - \sum_{i=1}^{n} \frac{(2i-1)}{n} \left[\ln F_0(X_{(i)}) + \ln(1 - F_0(X_{(n+1-i)}))\right].$$ Under $H_0$, $A^2$ has an asymptotic distribution that does not depend on the specific normal parameters (when they are estimated). The AD test generally has higher power than the KS/Lilliefors test for detecting heavy-tailed alternatives, and is widely used in reliability engineering and finance.

4.17.4 Jarque–Bera Test

The Jarque–Bera (JB) test exploits the fact that a normal distribution has skewness $\gamma_1 = 0$ and excess kurtosis $\gamma_2 - 3 = 0$. The test statistic: $$\mathrm{JB} = \frac{n}{6}\left[\hat{\gamma}_1^2 + \frac{(\hat{\gamma}_2 - 3)^2}{4}\right],$$ where $\hat{\gamma}_1 = m_3/m_2^{3/2}$ and $\hat{\gamma}_2 = m_4/m_2^2$ are the sample skewness and kurtosis based on central moments $m_k = \frac{1}{n}\sum(X_i - \bar{X})^k$. Under $H_0$, $\mathrm{JB} \overset{d}{\to} \chi^2_2$ as $n \to \infty$. The JB test is powerful for large $n$ and is standard in econometrics and time series analysis.

Comparison of Normality Tests
TestSensitive toBest useLimitation
Shapiro–WilkAll departures$n \leq 5000$, general useUnavailable in base R for $n > 5000$
Lilliefors (KS)Centre and tailsSimple explanation, large $n$Less powerful than SW for small $n$
Anderson–DarlingTail departuresFinance, reliabilityRequires family-specific tables
Jarque–BeraSkewness, kurtosisEconometrics, large $n$Poor power for small $n$ ($< 50$)
Warning · Over-Reliance on Normality Tests

Two pathologies arise when using formal normality tests to decide whether to use parametric procedures:

  • Small $n$: The normality test has low power and will rarely reject, even for highly non-normal distributions. But the t-test is sensitive to non-normality for small $n$. Passing a normality test with $n = 10$ provides almost no protection against non-normality.
  • Large $n$: The normality test has very high power and will detect tiny, practically irrelevant departures (e.g., a slight skewness of 0.05). But the t-test is highly robust for large $n$ due to the CLT. Abandoning a t-test for a non-parametric test because of a marginally significant normality test with $n = 10{,}000$ is usually unnecessary.

Recommendation: Use Q-Q plots to assess the nature and severity of departures visually. Use normality tests mainly as rough screening tools. Focus on whether the departure is substantial enough to invalidate the downstream inference you plan to use.

4.18 Decision Framework and Model Diagnostics

Hypothesis testing in practice requires navigating a sequence of decisions: which test is appropriate, which assumptions need checking, and how to proceed when assumptions are violated. This section integrates the tests and diagnostics from earlier sections into a unified decision framework.

4.18.1 Test Selection Framework

The choice of test depends on the outcome type, number of groups, sample size, distributional assumptions, and the specific null hypothesis. The table below consolidates the chapter’s main tests:

Test Selection Table
ObjectiveGroupsParametric TestNon-parametric Alternative
Compare mean to $\mu_0$1z-test / one-sample t-testWilcoxon signed-rank, sign test
Compare two means2 indep.Welch’s t-testMann–Whitney U
Compare paired means2 pairedPaired t-testWilcoxon signed-rank
Compare 3+ means$k > 2$One-way ANOVA (F-test)Kruskal–Wallis
Compare variance to $\sigma_0^2$1Chi-square testBootstrap CI for variance
Compare two variances2F-test / BartlettLevene, Brown–Forsythe
Test proportion1z-test for $p$Exact binomial
Compare two proportions2Two-sample z-testFisher’s exact test
Test independence (categorical)—Pearson $\chi^2$, G-testFisher’s exact (small $n$)
Test distributional fit—Chi-square GOF, Kolmogorov–SmirnovAnderson–Darling
Nested model comparison—Likelihood ratio test (Wilks)Permutation test

4.18.2 Layered Diagnostic Protocol

Before running any hypothesis test, apply the following diagnostic protocol:

  1. Plot the data. Histograms, boxplots, and scatter plots reveal outliers, multimodality, skewness, heteroscedasticity, and trend. No statistical test replaces careful data visualization.
  2. Assess distributional assumptions graphically. Q-Q plots for normality; index plots of residuals for independence; scatter of residuals vs. fitted values for homoscedasticity.
  3. Check for outliers. Outliers have disproportionate influence on means, variances, and test statistics. Identify them using boxplot rules ($|x - \tilde{x}| > 1.5 \times \mathrm{IQR}$), Grubbs’ test, or standardized residuals $|e_i/s| > 3$. Investigate their origin.
  4. Assess independence. For time series or spatially structured data, check autocorrelation (ACF plots) or spatial autocorrelation (Moran’s I).
  5. State hypotheses and significance level before computing the test statistic. This is essential to avoid p-hacking.
  6. Compute the test statistic, p-value, effect size, and confidence interval.
  7. Report all four quantities. A statistically significant result is not necessarily scientifically meaningful; the effect size and CI quantify the magnitude and precision of the result.

4.18.3 Reporting Best Practices

Reporting Standards for Hypothesis Tests
  • Never report just “$p < 0.05$.” Report the exact p-value, the test statistic with degrees of freedom, the effect size, and a confidence interval. Example: “The treatment significantly reduced LDL cholesterol ($t_{29} = 3.14$, $p = 0.004$, Cohen’s $d = 0.57$, 95% CI: [0.19, 0.95] standardized units).”
  • Distinguish statistical and practical significance. A difference of 0.001 units may be highly significant with $n = 10{,}000$ but scientifically trivial. The CI quantifies whether the effect size is practically important.
  • Pre-register your analysis. Specify hypotheses, primary endpoint, sample size, $\alpha$, and the analysis plan before seeing data. This prevents HARKing (Hypothesizing After Results are Known) and p-hacking.
  • Distinguish confirmatory from exploratory analyses. Pre-specified primary endpoints are confirmatory; secondary or post-hoc analyses are exploratory and should be labeled as such, with appropriate multiple comparison corrections.
  • Report analysis code and data. Computational reproducibility requires sharing the code used to produce each result, the exact software versions, and (where possible) the data.
Data Science Application · The Complete Responsible Testing Pipeline

End-to-end hypothesis testing workflow for production data science:

  1. Define the business question as a statistical hypothesis before any data is collected or examined: e.g., “Does personalized onboarding increase 30-day retention by at least 3 percentage points?”
  2. Power analysis: Use historical data to estimate baseline rate and variance. Compute required sample size for 80% power at $\alpha = 0.05$.
  3. Pre-registration: Document the primary metric, statistical test, $\alpha$, and analysis plan in a product specification or analysis plan document, signed off before launch.
  4. Design the experiment with proper randomization, holdout groups, and guard rails (sample ratio mismatch checks, novelty effect monitoring, network effect detection).
  5. Run the experiment for the pre-specified duration. Do not stop early based on peeking unless using a valid sequential testing procedure.
  6. Analyze per the pre-registered plan. Compute primary metric p-value, CI, and effect size. Separate secondary metric analysis (exploratory) with appropriate caveats.
  7. Report the result including the p-value, CI, effect size, sample size, and any deviations from the analysis plan. Make a deployment recommendation based on statistical and business considerations jointly.

✦ Chapter Summary

This chapter developed the theory and practice of hypothesis testing from first principles. We began with the logical structure of null hypothesis significance testing, characterized its error types and power function, and derived the Neyman–Pearson Lemma as the optimal foundation for all test construction. We then built upward through the major classes of exact and approximate tests — for means (z and t), variances (chi-square and F), proportions, Poisson rates, and general composite hypotheses (GLRT/Wilks). We concluded with tools for diagnosing when tests are appropriate (Q-Q plots, rootograms, normality tests) and frameworks for controlling error rates when testing many hypotheses simultaneously.

NULL HYPOTHESIS FRAMEWORK

A hypothesis test maps data to a binary reject/retain decision via a test statistic $T$ and rejection region $C$. The null $H_0: \theta \in \Theta_0$ is rejected when $T \in C$. Size is $\sup_{\theta \in \Theta_0} \mathbb{P}_\theta(T \in C)$; a level-$\alpha$ test has size $\leq \alpha$.

p-VALUE

The p-value $p = \mathbb{P}_{H_0}(T \geq t_\text{obs})$ is the probability of results as extreme or more extreme under $H_0$. Under a true $H_0$, $p \sim \text{Uniform}(0,1)$. Reject $H_0$ at level $\alpha$ iff $p \leq \alpha$. The p-value is not the probability $H_0$ is true.

NEYMAN–PEARSON LEMMA

For simple $H_0$ vs. $H_1$, the most powerful level-$\alpha$ test rejects when $\Lambda = \mathcal{L}(\theta_1)/\mathcal{L}(\theta_0) > k$. This generates UMP tests for one-sided alternatives in exponential families via the MLR property, but UMP tests rarely exist for two-sided alternatives.

t-TESTS

When $\sigma^2$ is unknown, $T = (\bar{X}-\mu_0)/(S/\sqrt{n}) \sim t_{n-1}$ exactly under normality (Cochran). Welch’s test handles unequal variances via the Satterthwaite df. The paired t-test reduces two-sample to one-sample by differencing, eliminating between-subject variance.

GLRT AND WILKS’ THEOREM

The GLRT statistic $-2\log\Lambda = -2[\ell(\hat{\theta}_0) - \ell(\hat{\theta})]$ converges to $\chi^2_r$ under $H_0$ (Wilks), where $r = \dim(\Theta) - \dim(\Theta_0)$ constraints are imposed. This gives asymptotic critical values for any composite test in regular parametric models.

POWER AND SAMPLE SIZE

Power $\pi(\theta) = \mathbb{P}_\theta(\text{reject})$ depends on $\alpha$, effect size $d = \delta/\sigma$, and $n$. The sample size formula $n = \sigma^2(z_\alpha + z_\beta)^2/\delta^2$ links all four quantities. Underpowered studies produce inflated effect estimates (winner’s curse) in published results.

MULTIPLE TESTING

Testing $m$ nulls simultaneously inflates Type I errors. Bonferroni ($p_j \leq \alpha/m$) controls FWER under any dependence; Holm is uniformly more powerful. Benjamini–Hochberg controls FDR $\leq \alpha$ under PRDS, enabling more discoveries at the cost of tolerating a small fraction of false positives.

GOODNESS-OF-FIT TESTS

Pearson’s $\chi^2 = \sum (O-E)^2/E \sim \chi^2_{k-1-s}$ tests distributional fit. The G-test $G = 2\sum O\ln(O/E)$ is the GLRT equivalent. Independence in $r \times c$ tables uses $\chi^2_{(r-1)(c-1)}$. All require $E_j \geq 5$ for adequate chi-square approximation.

NON-PARAMETRIC TESTS

The Mann–Whitney U (Wilcoxon rank-sum) tests $H_0: P(X>Y) = 1/2$ without distributional assumptions; Wilcoxon signed-rank tests symmetry around zero. Under normality both have ARE $= 3/\pi \approx 0.955$ vs. t-tests; for heavy-tailed distributions, ARE $> 1$.

DIAGNOSTIC TOOLS

Normal Q-Q plots reveal distributional shape: S-curves indicate heavy tails; bow-shapes indicate light tails. Hanging rootograms compare observed vs. expected counts on the $\sqrt{\cdot}$ scale. Shapiro–Wilk is most powerful for $n \leq 50$; for large $n$, use Q-Q plots rather than formal tests which detect trivial departures.

Exercises

Exercise 4.1  ·  NP LEMMA AND UMP TESTS
Optimal Detection of Signal in Exponential Noise

Let $X_1, \ldots, X_n \overset{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim} \mathrm{Exponential}(\lambda)$, where $\mathbb{E}[X_i] = 1/\lambda$.

(a) Using the Neyman–Pearson Lemma, derive the most powerful test of $H_0: \lambda = \lambda_0$ vs. $H_1: \lambda = \lambda_1 > \lambda_0$ at level $\alpha$. Show all steps of the likelihood ratio derivation and simplify the rejection region to a condition on $\bar{X}$.

(b) Prove that the same test is UMP for the one-sided alternative $H_1: \lambda > \lambda_0$ by showing the rejection region does not depend on the specific value of $\lambda_1$.

(c) Let $n = 20$, $\lambda_0 = 1$, $\alpha = 0.05$. Compute the exact critical value $c$ such that $\mathbb{P}_{\lambda_0}(\bar{X} < c) = 0.05$, using the fact that $2n\lambda_0 \bar{X} \sim \chi^2_{2n}$.

(d) [Data Science] In anomaly detection, interarrival times of network packets are often modeled as Exponential. Describe how the UMP test from (a) would be deployed as a real-time alert system, and what determines the trade-off between false positive rate and detection latency.

Exercise 4.2  ·  TWO-SAMPLE t-TESTS AND POWER
Clinical Trial Design for a Cardiovascular Drug

A pharmaceutical company designs a Phase III trial comparing a new drug to placebo. Primary endpoint: systolic blood pressure (SBP) reduction after 12 weeks. From Phase II: $\sigma \approx 15$ mmHg (both arms). Minimum clinically meaningful difference: $\delta = 5$ mmHg.

(a) State the hypotheses. Derive the formula for the power of the two-sample Welch t-test as a function of $n$ (per arm), $\delta$, $\sigma$, and $\alpha$. Assume equal group sizes.

(b) Find the minimum $n$ per arm to achieve 90% power at $\alpha = 0.025$ (one-sided). Use $z_{0.025} = 1.96$, $z_{0.10} = 1.282$.

(c) After enrollment, the treatment arm ($n_T = 180$) shows $\bar{X}_T = 148.3$ mmHg and $S_T = 14.2$; placebo ($n_P = 175$) shows $\bar{X}_P = 153.7$ mmHg and $S_P = 16.1$. Compute the Welch t-statistic, Welch–Satterthwaite df, and p-value. State the decision at $\alpha = 0.025$.

(d) Twelve secondary endpoints are also tested. Apply the Benjamini–Hochberg procedure at FDR $= 0.05$ to ordered p-values: $0.002, 0.008, 0.021, 0.035, 0.044, 0.08, 0.12, 0.19, 0.27, 0.43, 0.68, 0.91$. How many secondary hypotheses are rejected? What is the BH threshold for the largest rejected p-value?

Exercise 4.3  ·  GLRT AND WILKS’ THEOREM
Testing Structural Breaks in a Linear Model

Suppose $Y_i = \beta_0 + \beta_1 x_i + \varepsilon_i$ for $i = 1, \ldots, n$ with $\varepsilon_i \overset{\mathrm{iid}}{\sim} N(0, \sigma^2)$. Researchers suspect a structural break at a known time $t^*$: the slope may differ between the first $m$ and last $n - m$ observations.

(a) Write out the restricted model (common slope $\beta_1$, unrestricted intercepts) and the full model (separate slopes $\beta_1^{(1)}, \beta_1^{(2)}$, separate intercepts). Identify the constraint matrix and the degrees of freedom $r$ of the Wilks chi-square.

(b) Show that the GLRT is equivalent to a Chow F-test and express the F-statistic in terms of the residual sums of squares (RSS) from the restricted and unrestricted models: $F = [(RSS_r - RSS_u)/r] / [RSS_u/(n-2k)]$, where $k$ is the number of parameters per regime.

(c) Suppose $n = 100$, $m = 50$. The restricted model has $RSS_r = 412.3$ and the full model has $RSS_u = 385.6$. Compute the F-statistic, state the null distribution, and determine whether you reject at $\alpha = 0.05$.

(d) [Data Science] In practice, $t^*$ is often unknown. Explain why testing structural breaks at all possible $t^*$ and taking the maximum F-statistic inflates the Type I error, and name the correction that produces valid asymptotic critical values in this setting.

Exercise 4.4  ·  MULTIPLE TESTING IN GWAS
Genome-Wide Association Studies and FDR Control

A GWAS study tests $m = 500{,}000$ SNPs for association with a binary disease outcome. SNP associations are tested using a chi-square test of independence at each locus. The study nominally uses $\alpha = 5 \times 10^{-8}$ (the conventional genome-wide significance threshold).

(a) Show that $\alpha = 5 \times 10^{-8}$ is approximately the Bonferroni-corrected threshold for FWER $= 0.05$ with $m = 10^6$ independent tests. Why is $m = 10^6$ used rather than the actual number of SNPs tested?

(b) The study identifies 47 SNPs with $p < \alpha_{\mathrm{Bonf}} = 5 \times 10^{-8}$ and an additional 312 with $5 \times 10^{-8} \leq p < 5 \times 10^{-6}$. Apply the BH procedure to control FDR at $\alpha = 0.05$ across all 500,000 tests. Would the BH procedure reject more or fewer hypotheses than the Bonferroni threshold? Explain the trade-off.

(c) Suppose 40% of tested SNPs are in LD (linkage disequilibrium) with a nearby SNP, inducing positive correlations between test statistics. Does the BH procedure still control FDR in this setting? State the relevant theorem and its conditions.

(d) [Data Science] Describe the concept of Storey’s q-value as an alternative to the BH procedure. How does estimating $\pi_0 = m_0/m$ improve power relative to the BH procedure when many of the $m$ hypotheses are truly false?

Exercise 4.5  ·  GOODNESS-OF-FIT AND COUNT MODELS
Fitting Count Distributions to E-Commerce Demand Data

An e-commerce platform models daily order counts per SKU (stock-keeping unit) over $n = 250$ days. Observed counts: $O_0 = 32$ (zero orders), $O_1 = 58$, $O_2 = 71$, $O_3 = 47$, $O_4 = 25$, $O_5 = 11$, $O_{6+} = 6$. The MLE of $\lambda$ under a Poisson fit is $\hat{\lambda} = \bar{X}$.

(a) Compute $\hat{\lambda}$ from the observed data. Compute the expected counts $E_k = n \cdot e^{-\hat{\lambda}} \hat{\lambda}^k / k!$ for $k = 0, 1, \ldots, 5$ and $E_{6+} = n - \sum_{k=0}^5 E_k$. Report a table of $O_k$, $E_k$, and $(O_k - E_k)^2/E_k$.

(b) Compute the Pearson chi-square statistic $X^2 = \sum (O_k - E_k)^2/E_k$. What are the degrees of freedom? State the decision at $\alpha = 0.05$.

(c) The dispersion test statistic is $D = (n-1)S^2/\bar{X}$. Compute $S^2$ and $D$. Does the Poisson model appear adequate from a dispersion perspective? Which model would you suggest as an alternative if $D$ is large?

(d) Sketch what the hanging rootogram would look like if the data were overdispersed relative to Poisson. Describe specifically which bins would show bars above and below the baseline, and in what pattern.
